title: Meta enters AI image model race in bid to court advertisers and subscribers
blurb: Meta released Muse Image, an AI image-generation model available free in the Meta AI app, WhatsApp, and Instagram Stories, with heavier usage gated behind its new Meta One subscription. The model, Meta Superintelligence Labs' second release under Alexandr Wang, also powers Advantage Plus ad tools and lets Meta cut reliance on third-party providers like Midjourney, though internal benchmarks show it trailing OpenAI's GPT Image 2.

title: Inference chip startup SambaNova valued at $11B in $1B funding round
blurb: SambaNova raised $1B at an $11B valuation in a Series F round led by General Atlantic, with Intel Capital, Vista Equity Partners, and JPMorgan among the participants. The funding follows a $350M round in February and backs the SN50, an inference chip SambaNova says delivers over three times the throughput of Nvidia's B200 by placing SRAM memory next to processing circuits to cut data-movement delays.

title: Meta launches image generation model with coding, search capabilities
blurb: Meta released Muse Image, an image generation model that reasons before rendering and can call search and code-execution tools to fill in missing details or script complex visualizations. It is the second model from Meta Superintelligence Labs after the Muse Spark LLM, and it supports test-time compute, meaning more hardware yields higher-quality output.

title: Europe Posted Its Strongest Venture Funding Quarter In 4 Years As UK Gains, M&A Holds Up
blurb: Europe-based startups raised $24B in Q2, the region's strongest venture quarter in four years, up roughly a third quarter over quarter and two-thirds above Q2 2025. Four billion-dollar AI rounds drove the gains, while UK startups took more than $10B for their third-largest quarter on record, though European totals still trail North America's $392B H1 by a wide margin.

title: Sonos loses a decade of design talent as layoffs hit its top ranks
blurb: Sonos laid off a swath of its most senior product, design, and UX research leaders, including several 10-to-15-year veterans across design, hardware, and user research. CEO Tom Conrad framed the cuts, part of a roughly 3% reduction weighted toward the teams behind its troubled app, as a push for speed and fewer management layers rather than cost savings, and said they were unrelated to AI.

title: Apple starts testing cheaper Chinese RAM inside iPhones, but your pocket won’t feel the ease
blurb: Apple has begun testing DRAM from Chinese supplier CXMT, the world's fourth-largest memory maker, for iPhones and other devices sold in China. The move is unlikely to lower prices globally, since CXMT's output is already committed and supply-constrained for at least two years, and the chipmaker's spot on the Pentagon's Chinese Military Company list poses political risk for Apple.

title: OpenAI just upgraded ChatGPT voice mode in a major way, including CarPlay
blurb: OpenAI released GPT-Live, a new full-duplex voice model for ChatGPT that can listen and speak at the same time, letting users talk over it and interrupt mid-sentence. It ships today on iOS, Android, and web in two versions, with GPT-Live-1-mini as the default for free users and GPT-Live-1 for paid tiers, and it also upgrades the CarPlay voice experience.

title: Microsoft reportedly laid off half of id Software's employees during Xbox restructuring
blurb: Microsoft reportedly laid off roughly half of id Software's staff during what Xbox has called the most significant restructuring in its history. The Doom developer was among the hardest-hit studios in the cuts, which reached deep into Microsoft's first-party gaming ranks following the broader post-Activision consolidation.

title: Forget Google Chrome, Brave makes new official bypass to support uBlock Origin and more
blurb: Brave shipped an official workaround that keeps uBlock Origin and other Manifest V2 extensions fully functional, even as Google's Chrome phases out MV2 support. The move positions the Chromium-based browser as a refuge for ad-blocking holdouts, letting users keep the extensions that Chrome's MV3 transition is set to break.

title: AVX-512 support is reportedly returning with Intel's next-gen Nova Lake CPUs — Latest Linux kernel patches reveal P-cores and E-cores will gain native 512-bit execution
blurb: Intel will restore AVX-512 in its next-gen Nova Lake CPUs, with Linux kernel patches indicating native 512-bit execution on both P-cores and E-cores. The feature was dropped after 2021's hybrid Alder Lake design, whose E-cores couldn't run it and would crash any 512-bit thread the scheduler moved to them; unifying support across core types removes that scheduling hazard and matches AMD's full-width Zen 5 registers.

title: Kioxia and Sandisk sample world's densest 3D NAND — new 332-Layer beats Samsung’s 400-Layer NAND
blurb: Kioxia and Sandisk began sampling their 10th-generation BiCS 3D TLC NAND, a 332-layer design that reaches over 29 Gb/mm² of areal density and a 4,800 MT/s transfer rate. Aimed at PCIe 5.0 and 6.0 data center SSDs rather than consumer drives, the chips boost bit density 59% while cutting read latency roughly 10% and read energy 25%.

title: Former GitHub CEO’s startup Entire unveils its answer to the crush of AI coding agents
blurb: Entire, the startup from former GitHub CEO Thomas Dohmke, launched a preview of its distributed Git network that mirrors repositories across the US, Europe, and Australia so AI agents can clone and pull from a faster, closer copy. The company emerged in February with a $60M seed led by Felicis at a $300M valuation, and positions itself alongside coding agents like Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex rather than as a GitHub competitor.

title: PSA: Flip these two Instagram toggles now to stop people using your face
blurb: Meta launched Muse Image, an AI image model built into Instagram, and by default it opts every public profile into being used as source material for other people's generative remixes. Anyone can tag a public account in a prompt to generate images from its likeness, so users must dig into Settings, Sharing and Reuse to flip off separate Posts and Reels toggles to opt out.

title: GAO: DOE Is Prematurely Excluding Less Expensive Options for Nuclear Cleanup
blurb: A GAO report found the Department of Energy is ruling out cheaper cleanup methods for its radioactive waste sites too early in the process, before fully evaluating them. The watchdog warns that DOE's practice of defaulting costly disposal approaches could inflate an environmental liability already estimated at over $500B, and recommends the agency weigh less expensive alternatives before committing.

title: Behind Xbox's Big Layoffs, a Streaming Strategy That Failed
blurb: Microsoft's Xbox layoffs traced back to a failed bet on game streaming, according to Bloomberg reporting on the division's cuts. The company poured resources into cloud gaming and xCloud infrastructure that never reached the scale needed to justify the spend, leaving the hardware and studio teams to absorb the reductions.

title: China May Restrict Access to Its Most Powerful AI Models
blurb: Chinese authorities have held talks with Alibaba, ByteDance, and Z.ai about restricting foreign access to their most advanced AI models, including unreleased ones, Reuters reported. The move would reverse the open-weight strategy that drove China's global adoption despite trailing US models by seven months, and it mirrors Washington's own restrictions on Anthropic's Mythos and OpenAI's GPT-5.6 over security concerns.

title: Despite ‘misgivings,’ judge approves Elon Musk’s $1.5M SEC settlement
blurb: A federal judge approved a $1.5M penalty settling the SEC's lawsuit against Elon Musk over his delayed disclosure of a growing Twitter stake in 2022, despite writing that she had "significant misgivings" about the deal. Judge Sparkle Sooknanan said her review was limited to whether the settlement met minimum fairness standards, even after the SEC argued Musk's late disclosure saved him roughly $150M.

title: Fabless Korean startup wants to entice European inference chip-hungry enterprises
blurb: FuriosaAI, the South Korean chip startup that rejected an $800M Meta acquisition offer, is expanding into Europe with a deal to deploy its RNGD inference accelerators in Equinix's Lisbon data center. The 5nm, 180-watt PCIe cards give European enterprises a non-American inference option, and the sites double as showrooms as Furiosa pursues independence backed by a $250M Series C and a Broadcom partnership on its next-gen 2nm chip.

title: Data centers’ energy demand threatens Trump’s “Made in America” plan
blurb: AI data center demand is driving up electricity costs for US manufacturers, squeezing steelmakers and other Rust Belt factories that compete with data centers for power on the PJM grid. Capacity prices in PJM territory jumped from $28.92 to $329.17 per megawatt-day between 2024 and 2026, adding tens of millions in annual costs for steel producers and undercutting Trump's "Made in America" push.

title: US seeks cheaper hunter-killer drones after Iran destroys $1B worth of Reapers
blurb: The Pentagon's Defense Innovation Unit is soliciting industry pitches for large numbers of cheaper hunter-killer drones after Iran destroyed dozens of MQ-9 Reapers worth more than $1 billion. The notice calls current reliance on aircraft costing over $30 million each "unsustainable" against low-cost air defenses, and points to Ukraine's mass drone strikes on Russia as the model for overwhelming defenses despite heavy losses.

title: SCOTUS lets Texas enforce app store law that Big Tech calls "censorship regime"
blurb: The Supreme Court declined to reinstate an injunction against the Texas App Store Accountability Act, letting the state enforce age-verification rules on app stores while litigation continues. The law requires Apple and Google to verify users' ages and secure parental consent for minors; a tech lobby group calls it a "broad censorship regime," but the 5th Circuit, which hears oral arguments August 4, has signaled Texas is likely to win.

title: Meta is building its first big Canadian data center as AI expansion crosses the border
blurb: Meta is building its first Canadian data center, a 1-gigawatt facility in Alberta that will cost about $9B and take two to three years to complete. It's the company's 33rd data center and part of a capex push of up to $145B this year, a buildout investors have questioned as Meta trails OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google with no clear AI revenue outside ads.

title: OpenAI secures U.S. regulatory green light for GPT-5.6 rollout, Axios report says
blurb: OpenAI is preparing a broad release of GPT-5.6 as early as this week after clearing federal testing, Axios reported, though the White House told CNBC it gave no "green light" and said release decisions rest with companies. The move follows last month's phased debut of the Sol, Terra, and Luna models to trusted partners, and Anthropic's own week-long suspension of Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 under export controls.

title: Apple begins testing CXMT chips for devices sold in China, FT says
blurb: Apple has begun testing DRAM chips from China's state-backed CXMT for devices sold in China and is lobbying Washington to permit broader use of the supplier's products, the Financial Times reported. The move is geopolitically sensitive as the US works to contain Chinese chipmaking, and CXMT, now the world's fourth-largest DRAM producer, is targeting at least $4.3B in a Shanghai IPO.

title: Blue Origin seeks to raise $10B from external investors in first VC-led funding round
blurb: Blue Origin is in talks to raise $10B from outside investors at a valuation above $130B, its first VC-led round in 25 years. Coatue Management would lead with $4B and Jeff Bezos would add $2B of his own money, making the Bezos-founded rocket company one of the most valuable pure-play space firms, though still far behind SpaceX's trillion-dollar-plus market cap.

title: Tensordyne targets AI inference market with logarithmic math and Juniper-derived rack architecture
blurb: Tensordyne emerged from stealth with its first inference chip in production at TSMC, using a logarithmic number system that replaces power-hungry multiplier circuits with far smaller adders. The startup pairs its "Pareto" LogMath silicon with a Juniper Networks-derived rack architecture to challenge Nvidia on AI inference cost and efficiency, while keeping the math hidden behind a standard floating-point SDK.

title: Quantum startup Oratomic banks $300M to race straight to fault-tolerance
blurb: Oratomic raised $300M in Series A funding to build a fault-tolerant, neutral-atom quantum computer, co-led by ARCH Venture Partners, Spark Capital, and Khosla Ventures. Working with Caltech, the four-month-old startup argues a cryptographically relevant machine needs only about 10,000 reconfigurable qubits, a far smaller target than rivals like IQM chasing 1 million.

title: Amazon launches $25B bond sale to fund AI infrastructure
blurb: Amazon launched a $25B bond sale across eight tranches, with maturities from three to 40 years, to help fund its AI infrastructure buildout. The raise supports a capital budget guided toward roughly $200B this year, up from $131B in 2025, as data centers, chips, and AWS gear outpace the cash Amazon generates.

title: Meta CTO says employee-tracking data landed ‘where it wasn’t supposed to go’
blurb: Meta CTO Andrew Bosworth said the company halted its keystroke-logging AI project, the Model Capability Initiative, after a researcher moved sensitive employee data to an internal location where it became readable across the organization. Bosworth denied any external breach and said Meta is locking down the program to investigate, following a backlash in which more than 1,600 employees petitioned against the mandatory tracking software.

title: The satellite industry’s most expensive problem isn’t in orbit
blurb: The satellite industry's biggest bottleneck to global connectivity is billing, not orbital hardware. Despite 275+ partnerships between satellite and mobile network operators, most carriers still settle payments through a clearing standard built in 1991 for minutes, messages and data, leaving satellite roaming traffic stuck in incompatible workarounds that keep volumes small and returns low.

title: The government’s options to address strained spaceports
blurb: A NASA Inspector General report warns that Kennedy Space Center and Wallops could hit launch capacity as early as 2028, with aging 1960s-era infrastructure like Launch Complex 39's power system unable to support planned Starship, New Glenn, and Vulcan flights. Officials estimate KSC alone needs at least $1B in upgrades, and the May 28 New Glenn explosion at Cape Canaveral underscored how strained US spaceports have become as launch demand outpaces capacity.

title: Rocket Lab sees Electron gains, Neutron delays in first half of 2026
blurb: Rocket Lab signed 36 new launch contracts in Q1 2026, more missions than it flew all of 2025, and posted record revenue of $200.3 million, up 63.5% year over year. Electron and HASTE drove the surge while the larger Neutron rocket slipped further behind schedule, leaving a combined backlog past 70 missions and Neutron's debut still pending.

title: Waymo will soon go fully autonomous in four more cities
blurb: Waymo will remove human safety supervisors from its robotaxis in San Diego, Las Vegas, Tampa, and Denver, expanding fully driverless operations to more than 10 US cities. The rides will start with Waymo employees before opening to the public, as the Alphabet unit also begins testing in London and ends its Uber partnership in Phoenix.

title: OpenAI's next-generation ChatGPT Voice will make Advanced Voice Mode look outdated
blurb: OpenAI is testing a next-generation ChatGPT voice that listens, thinks, and speaks simultaneously, dropping the turn-based walkie-talkie model.

title: China alleges that Claude Code contains backdoors, calls mechanism 'a serious threat' — Gov't claims Claude sends sensitive information to remote servers without consent
blurb: China's National Vulnerability Database labeled Claude Code a "serious threat," alleging the tool sent user location and identity data to remote servers without consent and urging users to uninstall or update. Anthropic engineer Thariq Shihipar said the tracking was a June experiment to curb account abuse and distillation by unauthorized resellers, and that it would be rolled back, even though Claude Code is not approved for use in China.

title: Samsung chip division's single-year profits beat its past 40 years of profits, combined, due to increased memory and storage prices — Samsung passes Nvidia to become most profitable company in the world, notches 19x quarterly increase in profit
blurb: Samsung's chip division expects its 2026 operating profit to exceed everything the unit has earned across roughly 40 years in the memory business, DS president Kim Yong-Kwan told staff. AI-driven demand pushed DRAM and NAND prices sharply higher, lifting quarterly operating profit 19x to $58.5 billion and passing Nvidia's $53.54 billion to make Samsung the most profitable tech company for the period.

title: QuantumDiamonds raises €91M, becomes the first startup approved for manufacturing funding under the EU Chips Act
blurb: QuantumDiamonds raised €91M to scale its quantum-based chip inspection technology, becoming the first startup to win manufacturing funding under the EU Chips Act. The Munich company's round splits into €15M in equity led by World Fund and €76M in non-dilutive government funding, a category previously reserved for established players like GlobalFoundries and STMicroelectronics.

title: Quantum Software Engineering in Practice: FPGA and AI Integration for Quantum Certification
blurb: QAccCert is a hybrid framework that certifies quantum entanglement by combining FPGAs, AI-guided optimization, and Qiskit simulations to verify devices produce valid entangled states despite noise and decoherence. Using LLM-guided parameter search, the system reached 99.94% of the theoretical CHSH inequality maximum in simulation, outperforming random search and pointing toward scalable certification for real NISQ hardware.

title: TeraWulf shares surge on $19B Anthropic AI infrastructure lease deal
blurb: TeraWulf signed a 20-year lease with Anthropic worth roughly $19B in contracted revenue, sending its shares up 13% Monday. The deal covers 401MW of capacity at the company's Kentucky data campus, with initial buildout in late 2027, while TeraWulf sold its 50.1% stake in a Texas joint venture to Fluidstack for about $450M to fund wholly owned projects.

title: I let React Compiler handle memoization: Here's what actually broke
blurb: React Compiler v1.0 shipped as stable in October 2025, promising to automate the memoization that `useMemo` and `useCallback` handle by hand. This walkthrough enables it on a production Next.js codebase and documents what broke: most components compiled cleanly, but the failures were subtle enough to ship unnoticed without tests, and the compiler silently opted some components out of optimization with no explanation.

title: Companies hire more after AI adoption
blurb: Ramp's Economics Lab published a working paper finding that U.S. firms grow headcount 10% in the two years after adopting AI, with entry-level roles growing 12% at the heaviest adopters. The gains hold only for high-intensity adopters and skew toward firms that were already larger, venture-backed, and engineering-heavy, so AI's hiring boost is concentrated rather than broad-based.

title: With EU backing, QuantumDiamonds aims to speed up chip manufacturing
blurb: QuantumDiamonds, a spinout from the Technical University of Munich, raised €15M in equity led by World Fund and secured €76M in non-dilutive German state funding to build a semiconductor testing facility in Munich. Its quantum-sensing hardware compresses chip defect detection from weeks into a two-minute inspection that runs without stopping production lines, which the startup says can save foundries and memory makers hundreds of millions of dollars.

title: Google Photos adds a new AI ‘Video Remix’ tool
blurb: Google Photos added a new AI Video Remix tool that edits and transforms clips in seconds using Gemini Omni, Google's recently released "create anything from any input" model. Available in the Create tab, it applies cinematic relighting, background swaps, and artistic styles like watercolor and oil paint, rolling out today to Google AI Plus, Pro, and Ultra subscribers across the US and 13 other countries.

title: Meta wants its AI glasses to seem less creepy. Its AI strategy says otherwise.
blurb: Meta will disable its AI glasses' camera if the recording LED is tampered with, even as it tests prototypes that continuously record audio and photos.

title: Ocean rift zone saw spreading happen in a sudden burst
blurb: French scientists remotely monitored a major seafloor spreading event on the Australian–Antarctic plate boundary in 2024, just two months after installing seabed equipment at a remote rift halfway between Australia and Madagascar. The data shows most of the spreading happened in a short burst, and some key movements occurred without any detectable seismic activity, giving researchers a first detailed look at the process rather than just its outcome.

title: Venus Aerospace Raises $91M Series B to Scale RDRE Production
blurb: Venus Aerospace raised $91M in a Series B led by Mercury Fund to scale production of its rotating detonation rocket engine (RDRE) for defense and commercial space missions. The company, which flew the first US RDRE-powered test flight in May 2025, says its engine runs 15% more efficient than conventional designs and will use the capital to expand its team, build larger production capacity, and fund follow-on demonstration flights.

title: Venus Aerospace raises $91 million to scale rocket engine technology
blurb: Venus Aerospace raised $91M in a Series B led by Mercury Fund to scale production of its rotating detonation rocket engine, which generates thrust from a continuously rotating detonation wave rather than conventional combustion. The 3D-printed, reusable engine targets hypersonic weapons, space launch, and lunar landers, and follows Venus's May flight test of a 2,000-pound-thrust RDRE from Spaceport America.

title: Apple now testing DRAM chips from banned Chinese memory supplier, per report
blurb: Apple has begun testing DRAM chips from CXMT, a Chinese memory maker on the Pentagon's blacklist over alleged PLA ties, for iPhones sold in China, per the Financial Times. The move is part of Apple's broader lobbying push to loosen US restrictions on Chinese suppliers as soaring memory prices force price hikes, though lawmakers warn it could strengthen state-backed competitors.

title: PSA: macOS 28 will drop support for encrypted Mac OS Extended volumes
blurb: Apple said macOS 28 will drop support for encrypted Mac OS Extended (HFS+) volumes, requiring users to decrypt or reformat affected external and legacy drives to keep using them. The change continues Apple's long migration to APFS, and starting with macOS 26 Macs will flag incompatible encrypted volumes by name, with reformatting to APFS as the recommended fix.

title: NHTSA calls out autonomous cars for interfering with first responders
blurb: NHTSA gave autonomous vehicle makers until the end of July to fix a "clear pattern" of driverless cars interfering with law enforcement and other first responders. The agency cited repeated cases of vehicles freezing and blocking ambulances, fire trucks, and stations, and officials in San Francisco and Austin reported Waymo cars committing more traffic violations and failing to recognize responders' hand signals.

title: SiPearl's long-awaited Rhea CPU finally gets in the lab, opening the door for Europe's first sovereign HPC CPU — 'availability of Rhea1 is scheduled for end of 2026' SiPearl VP says, following long development process
blurb: SiPearl received first silicon of its Rhea1 HPC CPU in mid-May and has entered a 12-week bring-up process, with the chip reportedly working as designed and needing no respin. The 80-core Arm Neoverse V1 processor pairs 64GB of on-package HBM2E with up to 2TB of DDR5 per socket, and is scheduled for availability in late 2026 as Europe's first sovereign high-performance computing CPU.

title: Power company hikes data center bills by 30%, cuts residential electricity costs by 1.3% — Oregon approves change through POWER Act, pushes developments using more than 20 Megawatts of power to pay their fair share
blurb: Oregon's Public Utility Commission unanimously approved a 29.7% rate hike for large power users like data centers and crypto miners, while cutting residential electricity costs by 1.3%. Enacted under the state's POWER Act, the new rate class targets developments drawing more than 20 MW, shifting grid-expansion costs onto the data centers driving the buildout rather than households.

title: South Korea's $880 billion chip and AI plan faces big power and water challenges — a single megacluster requires a quarter of Seoul's total power demand
blurb: South Korea launched an ₩1,350 trillion ($880 billion) 10-year plan for semiconductors, AI data centers, and robotics, but the power and water infrastructure lags years behind the fabs. The Yongin megacluster alone will need 15 to 16 GW at full operation, roughly a quarter of Seoul-metropolitan demand, against local supply of about 1.9 GW, and KEPCO's transmission fix isn't targeted for completion until 2036.

title: Dev ports Linux to Atari's notorious Jaguar console from 1993 — the first 64-bit console features 2MB of RAM, 13.3 MHz CPU, and Tom and Jerry co-processors; the Jag was notoriously difficult to program and flopped
blurb: A Spanish developer known as cakehonolulu ported Linux to the Atari Jaguar, the notoriously hard-to-program 1993 console that shipped with 2MB of RAM, a 13.3 MHz Motorola 68000, and custom Tom and Jerry co-processors. The port leaned on Linux's existing 68k support and the uClinux project to run on the MMU-less hardware, booting a working kernel and BusyBox shell on both real cartridges and emulators.

title: PlayStation can delete all your digital games after 3 years of inactivity (EU)
blurb: PlayStation's European terms of service let Sony close any account inactive for 36 months, permanently deleting every digitally purchased game tied to it. The clause has drawn scrutiny alongside Sony's plan to end physical PlayStation discs in 2028, sharpening a long-running question about whether buyers own digital games or merely license access that can be revoked.

title: Europe's company websites are mostly served by US vendors
blurb: A study of 19,450 European company websites found that US-headquartered vendors serve the majority of primary web presences in the UK (67.5%) and Netherlands (53.6%), and the plurality in Italy, Spain, and France. Cloudflare is the single largest infrastructure vendor in all seven markets sampled, ahead of every other US provider and every European host, with only Germany and Poland's dense domestic hosting industries as exceptions.

title: Broadcom, Apple Extend Tie-Up to 2031 With New Custom Chips
blurb: Broadcom and Apple extended their supply partnership through 2031, with Broadcom set to provide new custom chips alongside its existing wireless components. The multi-year deal locks in a key relationship for both companies as Apple advances its in-house silicon efforts while still relying on Broadcom for connectivity and specialized parts.

title: Anthropic Adds Enterprise Gateway to Simplify Claude Code Access on AWS and Google Cloud
blurb: Anthropic released a self-hosted Claude apps gateway that lets enterprises run Claude Code on Amazon Bedrock and Google Cloud without per-developer credential sprawl or manual setup. The stateless container, backed by PostgreSQL, centralizes identity via OIDC, policy enforcement, OTLP telemetry, provider routing with failover, and daily-to-monthly spend caps at the org, group, or individual level.

title: Meta is Quietly Launching Pocket, an App for Vibe-coding and Scrolling Small 'Gizmos'
blurb: Meta is quietly testing Pocket, an app for building and scrolling AI-generated "gizmos," small interactive apps and games made from prompts.

title: The power of collaboration: How we can reduce traffic congestion
blurb: Google Research shows coordinating even a small fraction of trips across 10 US cities measurably raised driving speeds and cut emissions.

title: Jeff Bezos just opened Blue Origin to outside investors for the first time in 26 years. Here’s what changed his mind
blurb: Blue Origin is raising roughly $10B from outside investors at a $130B pre-money valuation, the first outside capital Jeff Bezos has accepted since founding the company in 2000. Bezos will put in about $2B and Coatue Management roughly $4B, as Blue Origin scales its New Glenn rocket and chases lunar and defense contracts against a SpaceX now valued near $2 trillion after its record IPO.

title: Bethesda, id Software reportedly hit hard by Microsoft layoffs
blurb: Microsoft's 3,200-person Xbox layoffs hit first-party studios hard, with roughly 50% of Doom developer id Software let go, according to multiple reports and departing employees. Bethesda was also reportedly affected, and CEO Asha Sharma's announcement had emphasized platform and middle-management cuts rather than the roughly 90 id staffers who lost their jobs the same day the first Doom: The Dark Ages DLC shipped.

title: SK Hynix US Listing Is Seven Times Oversubscribed
blurb: SK Hynix's US listing drew orders more than seven times the shares on offer, signaling strong investor appetite despite recent volatility.

title: Iluvatar CoreX Raises $902 Million After 257% Stock Rally
blurb: Chinese GPU maker Iluvatar CoreX raised about $902 million in a Hong Kong share sale, capitalizing on a 257% stock rally since its January IPO. The Shanghai-based company designs AI accelerators positioned as domestic alternatives to Nvidia, and the raise gives it fresh capital as China pushes to build homegrown semiconductor capacity amid US export controls.

title: Meta Unveils an A.I. Image Generator
blurb: Meta released Muse Image, an AI image generator that creates realistic images for users inside Instagram and WhatsApp. The launch is Meta's latest push to close the gap with OpenAI, Google, and other rivals in the AI race, embedding generation directly into apps that already reach billions of users.

title: Refiant goes where rivals only promised with a 10 million-token AI model
blurb: Refiant launched Protea, a suite of long-context AI models led by a 10 million-token context window, roughly 7.5 million words or 15,000 pages, that the startup says ranks among the largest publicly available. The 2025-founded company, which earlier compressed OpenAI's GPT-OSS-120B to run on a 12GB MacBook Pro, claims Protea also fixes the "lost in the middle" problem and has already run an internal prototype at 100 million tokens.

title: Skyroot prepares for first orbital launch attempt
blurb: Skyroot Aerospace will attempt India's first private orbital launch as soon as July 12, sending its four-stage Vikram-1 rocket to a 450km orbit with a payload of domestic and international cubesats. The company, valued at $1.1 billion after a $60M round in May, plans up to two more Vikram-1 flights this year and an upgraded 550kg-capacity Vikram-1U in early 2027 as it scales toward monthly launches.

title: Siri AI can pull info from third-party apps in the latest developer beta
blurb: Apple's iOS 27 beta 3 lets Siri pull data from third-party apps, starting with electric vehicle battery levels surfaced from tools like Tessie and the Ford app. Early testers found it works through those integrations but not yet with the official Tesla app, and Siri prompts for permission before accessing each app's data.

title: Sheffield spinout just raised £5.2M to solve manufacturing problem that has kept MicroLED out of your pocket for a decade
blurb: Pixel-Flo, a University of Sheffield spinout, raised £5.25M to commercialize a continuous-flow mass-transfer process for microLED displays.

title: Microsoft fire idTech team at Id software
blurb: Microsoft laid off most of id Software's idTech engine team amid Xbox's 3,200-role restructure, ending a decades-long era.

title: Amazon without the knockoffs
blurb: Knockoff is a free browser extension that filters trademark-squat pseudo-brands out of Amazon search results, running entirely locally with no accounts or tracking.

title: PACE: A Proxy for Agentic Capability Evaluation
blurb: PACE predicts expensive agentic benchmark scores from a cheap subset of atomic evals, hitting under 4% error at under 1% of the cost.

title: xAI Is Dead. Long Live SpaceXAI
blurb: xAI has rebranded to SpaceXAI following SpaceX's acquisition of Musk's AI division, folding Grok into the space company's story.

title: Samsung to Get Jump on Apple's First Foldable With New Phones in July
blurb: Samsung will release its Galaxy Z Fold 8 and Z Flip 8 on July 22, moving ahead of Apple's first foldable iPhone expected later this year. The early launch gives Samsung a head start in defending its lead in the foldable market before Apple enters the category and reshapes competition.

title: Introducing Muse Image: Image Generation Built for Your World
blurb: Meta released Muse Image, the first image generation model from Meta Superintelligence Labs, now live in Meta AI. The model renders legible in-image text, edits existing photos, and pairs with Muse Spark to plan layouts and pull real-time web context, while powering 30-plus new Instagram Stories effects and image generation in WhatsApp chats in limited countries.

title: A Startup That Builds AI Agents Used One to Raise $100 Million
blurb: An enterprise AI agent startup raised $100M, and says it deployed one of its own agents to help run the fundraising process. The move doubles as a sales pitch: the company is betting that showing agents at work inside its own operations is more persuasive to enterprise buyers than benchmarks, as the market shifts from model quality to proof of real deployment.

title: The Language of AI Could Change How Humans Speak
blurb: Schneier argues that as humans encounter more AI-generated text, we will absorb the linguistic patterns of large language models, which are trained mostly on written and scripted speech and miss the bulk of unscripted conversation. He warns this could make us curter and more command-driven, narrow our vocabulary and sentence length toward the machine's 12-20 word average, and strip out the interruptions and leaps of logic that carry emotion.

title: Payloads used to dictate the terms of launch. That's finally changing.
blurb: SpaceX's Starship, with over 100 metric tons of payload capacity to low-Earth orbit, is inverting the usual relationship between rockets and satellites. Instead of launch vehicles being engineered to match satellite trends, US manufacturers, NASA, and the military are now redesigning payloads and missions around the abundant super-heavy-lift capacity, even as Starship remains experimental and far from proving Musk's biggest claims.

title: Mistral's Robostral Navigate: a state of the art robotics navigation model
blurb: Mistral released Robostral Navigate, an 8B embodied navigation model that moves robots through unseen environments from a single RGB camera and a plain-language instruction, with no LiDAR or depth sensors. It scores 76.6% success on the R2R-CE validation-unseen benchmark, beating the best single-camera approach by 9.7 points and the best depth or multi-camera system by 4.5 points.

title: Introducing GPT-Live
blurb: OpenAI released GPT-Live, a full-duplex voice model that listens and speaks at the same time, using backchannel cues like "mhmm" and pausing when the user needs to think. For complex queries it delegates to GPT-5.5 in the background while maintaining conversation flow, and it is rolling out in GPT-Live-1 and GPT-Live-1 mini versions to ChatGPT users globally today.

title: Grok 4.5
blurb: xAI released Grok 4.5, its strongest model to date, built for coding, agentic tasks, and knowledge work and trained alongside Cursor. The model runs at 80 tokens per second with roughly twice the token efficiency of comparable leading models, and now serves as the default in Grok Build for tasks like end-to-end app creation and multi-sheet Excel modeling.

title: Harness Engineering for Self-Improvement
blurb: Lilian Weng's latest post argues that a model's "harness," the runtime system that orchestrates how it plans, calls tools, manages context, and evaluates its own results, is as decisive for capability as the base model's raw intelligence. She frames recursive self-improvement around harness engineering, organizing recent work on auto-research, self-improving agents, and evolutionary program search under one question: how the deployment layer, not just the weights, drives each successor model. Coding agents like Claude Code and Codex are the proof points.

title: Gemma 4 Technical Report
blurb: Google released Gemma 4, a family of open-weight, natively multimodal models spanning 2.3B to 31B parameters across dense and Mixture-of-Experts architectures. The suite adds a thinking mode that generates reasoning traces before responding, a unified encoder-free 12B model that ingests raw audio and image patches, and long-context efficiency gains from a 5:1 local-to-global attention ratio and KV cache sharing.

title: AI Coding Tools Tricked Into Hacking Developer Machine via Decades-Old Technique
blurb: Wiz researchers demonstrated GhostApproval, an attack that tricks AI coding assistants including Claude Code, Cursor, Amazon Q Developer, Google Antigravity, Augment, and Windsurf into modifying sensitive system files via decades-old symlink following. The technique defeats human-in-the-loop safeguards because confirmation dialogs display the innocuous symlink path rather than its true target, and AWS, Google, and Cursor have shipped patches.

title: Separating signal from noise in coding evaluations
blurb: OpenAI audited SWE-Bench Pro and estimates that roughly 30% of its tasks are broken. The flaws fall into four categories: overly strict tests that reject functionally correct submissions, underspecified prompts that omit requirements enforced by hidden tests, low-coverage tests that let incomplete fixes pass, and misleading prompts that point models toward the wrong behavior. OpenAI argues flawed benchmarks distort capability measurements that inform deployment and safety decisions under its Preparedness Framework.

title: SWE-1.7 Reach Near GPT 5.5 and Opus Intelligence
blurb: Cognition released SWE-1.7, a coding model that reaches near GPT-5.5 and Opus intelligence at a fraction of the cost. Trained from a Kimi K2.7 base with an improved RL pipeline optimized for long-horizon asynchronous tasks, it is available today in Devin across Web, Desktop, and CLI, served via Cerebras at 1,000 tokens per second.

title: GhostApproval Symlink Flaws Could Let Malicious Repos Run Code in AI Coding Agents
blurb: Wiz disclosed GhostApproval, a class of symlink flaws in six AI coding assistants, including Claude Code, Cursor, Amazon Q Developer, Augment, Google Antigravity, and Windsurf, that lets a malicious repository redirect an approved file edit into sensitive targets like SSH keys or shell startup files. The approval box names a harmless file while the write lands elsewhere; three tools have shipped fixes, two have not, and Anthropic disputes it is a bug.

title: AI Coding Agents Found Triggering Endpoint Security Rules Built to Catch Attackers
blurb: Sophos analyzed a week of its own Windows endpoint telemetry and found that AI coding agents like Claude Code, Cursor, and OpenAI Codex are tripping behavioral detection rules built to catch human attackers, with credential access making up 56.2% of the blocked activity. The agents aren't malicious, but their routine work, such as using DPAPI to decrypt browser credentials or switching from certutil to bitsadmin when blocked, looks identical to living-off-the-land intrusion techniques.

title: GPT-5.6 Sol, along with Terra and Luna, will launch publicly this Thursday
blurb: OpenAI announced that GPT-5.6 Sol, along with the Terra and Luna models, will launch publicly this Thursday. The company is expanding preview access to the three models globally now ahead of the general release.

title: New HalluSquatting Attack Could Trick AI Coding Assistants Into Installing Botnet Malware
blurb: Researchers demonstrated HalluSquatting, an attack that exploits AI coding assistants' tendency to invent plausible-sounding names for nonexistent packages. Attackers record the fake names a model reliably hallucinates for trending resources, register those names on GitHub or plugin stores with hidden adversarial instructions, and wait for assistants to fetch the trap and run attacker-supplied code, reaching the same wrong name in up to 85% of repository requests.

title: Public GitHub Issue Could Trick GitHub Agentic Workflows Into Leaking Private Repo Data
blurb: Noma Security researchers demonstrated GitLost, an attack that tricks GitHub Agentic Workflows into leaking private repository contents through a normal-looking issue on a public repo, with no credentials or org access required. The technique relies on indirect prompt injection, and Noma found that prefixing malicious instructions with "Additionally" was enough to slip past GitHub's sandboxing and threat-detection guardrails.

title: Agon: Competitive Cross-Model RL with Implicit Rival Grading of Reasoning
blurb: Agon is a reinforcement learning method that trains two competing models to grade each other's reasoning, addressing GRPO's flaw of scoring only final answers rather than the thinking that produces them. Both models solve the same problem in alternating drafter and solver roles, each rewarded for out-solving a rival that has already seen its work, and on DeepMath's hard split with Qwen3 the approach doubles GRPO's pass@1.

title: OpenAI says it found widespread task issues in SWE-Bench Pro, estimates ~30% of tasks are broken, and retracts its earlier recommendation to adopt the benchmark (OpenAI)
blurb: OpenAI reported widespread problems in SWE-Bench Pro, estimating that roughly 30% of the benchmark's tasks are broken. The company retracted its earlier recommendation to adopt the benchmark, citing the flawed tasks as grounds for withholding endorsement until the issues are resolved.

title: Show HN: Microsoft releases Flint, a visualization language for AI agents
blurb: Microsoft released Flint, an open-source declarative language for visualizing the behavior and decision-making of AI agents. The tool renders agent execution as charts that trace steps, tool calls, and state transitions, giving developers a structured way to inspect and debug how agents reach their outputs.

title: GIFT: Geometry-Informed Low-precision Gradient Communication for LLM Pretraining
blurb: GIFT is a gradient communication method for LLM pretraining that transforms gradients into a near-isotropic coordinate space before quantizing them to low-precision formats like FP8 and NVFP4, reducing the direction-dependent distortion that degrades existing Euclidean quantization. On 64 NVIDIA GH200 Superchips, it cut end-to-end pretraining time for Llama-600M by 7.6% while improving downstream task preservation over direct FP8 communication, without changing the optimizer, training recipe, or communication collective.

title: AI Meets Cryptography 1: What AI Found in Cloudflare's Circl
blurb: zkSecurity pointed its AI audit agent, zkao, at Cloudflare's experimental CIRCL cryptography library and confirmed seven real bugs, ranging from a critical float64 precision loss in threshold RSA to a complete access-control break in attribute-based encryption. All seven have been fixed upstream. The post is the first in a series examining not just the bugs but how LLMs actually reason about cryptography, where they are sharp and where they are blind.

title: Top AI Agents Built to Catch Malicious Code Can Be Tricked Into Running It
blurb: The AI Now Institute published a proof-of-concept attack called "Friendly Fire" that tricks Anthropic's Claude Code and OpenAI's Codex into executing malicious code while auditing untrusted third-party projects for vulnerabilities. The attack works only when the agents run in autonomous modes that approve their own commands, and researchers argue the flaw is a design issue requiring a workflow change rather than a patch.

title: Benchmarking Coding Agents on Databricks’ Multi-Million Line Codebase
blurb: Databricks shared results from an internal benchmark that evaluates coding agents on real engineering tasks against its multi-million line codebase, spanning Python, Go, TypeScript, and Scala. Models and harnesses clustered into three capability tiers, and the analysis pushed Databricks to route common operational work to cheaper Haiku and GPT 5.4 Mini class models rather than defaulting to the most expensive ones.

title: OpenAI launches GPT-Live, a full-duplex voice upgrade that lets ChatGPT talk more like a person
blurb: OpenAI released GPT-Live, a pair of full-duplex voice models that replace Advanced Voice Mode and let ChatGPT listen and speak at the same time. GPT-Live-1 becomes the default for Go, Plus, and Pro users while GPT-Live-1 mini serves the free tier, rolling out globally on iOS, Android, and ChatGPT.com, with API access planned.

title: Search, Fail, Recover: A Training Framework for Correction-Aware Reasoning
blurb: Pyligent is a training and inference framework that represents reasoning as validated search over partial solution chains, teaching models to continue, finish, or backtrack when a branch fails. Compared with gold-only supervised fine-tuning, it improves solve rate by 72.7 points on a hidden directed graph task, 13 points on Blocksworld, and 14 to 27 points across Sudoku variants.

title: How Data Shapes RoPE Frequency Usage: From Positional Scale Matching to Length Generalization
blurb: This paper proposes a data-centered explanation for why transformers use RoPE positional frequencies non-uniformly, arguing that frequencies are selected to match the relative-distance structure of the training data, with the optimal frequency scaling as 1/W for a dependency profile of width W. The authors connect this frequency-matching principle to length generalization, showing that natural language's approximate self-similarity across positional scales explains why test-time frequency scaling supports long-context performance.

title: Max Out GRPO Signal: Adaptive Trace Prefix Control for Hard Reasoning Problems
blurb: AdaPrefix-GRPO addresses a failure mode where GRPO produces no gradient on a model's hardest problems, since a group with zero successful rollouts yields vanishing group-relative advantages. It prepends part of a reference solution as a difficulty knob, using a feedback controller to hold each problem's success rate near 50% before withdrawing assistance entirely, more than doubling held-out accuracy for a 0.6B model at matched FLOPs while roughly halving trace length.

title: The Key to Going Linear: Analysis-Driven Transformer Linearization
blurb: This paper isolates how state update design affects post hoc transformer linearization under a frozen backbone, showing that softmax's key-dependent rank-1 projections explain why delta-style networks beat gated accumulation. The authors add sink tokens, short convolutions, and fixed-budget cache routing, scaling to LLaMA and Qwen models up to 32B that outperform prior baselines on MMLU.

title: China Says It Has Found Security Vulnerabilities in Anthropic’s Claude Code
blurb: China's Cyberspace Administration said several versions of Anthropic's Claude Code contain security vulnerabilities that can transmit sensitive data, including user location and identity, to remote servers without consent. Regulators attributed the leakage to a built-in monitoring mechanism in the coding tool, a claim that adds friction to Anthropic's standing in the Chinese market.

title: Beware of Agentic Botnets: Scalable Untargeted Promptware Attacks via Universal and Transferable Adversarial HalluSquatting
blurb: This paper introduces adversarial hallucination squatting, an attack that exploits LLMs' tendency to hallucinate resource identifiers by pre-registering the fake repository and skill names that models predictably invent, then hosting adversarial prompts there. The authors report hallucinated resource generation rates up to 85% for repository cloning and 100% for skill installation, and show the technique transfers across foundational models to achieve remote code execution against production agents with integrated terminals.

title: [AINews] SpaceXAI launches Grok 4.5, first Opus-class model post Cursor acquisition
blurb: xAI released Grok 4.5, its first model trained specifically for coding and agents, in partnership with Cursor and positioned on capability-per-dollar rather than benchmark supremacy. The company describes it as roughly comparable to Opus 4.7 but faster and more token-efficient, and Cursor made it available in-product with double usage for the first week.

title: JEDEC releases new SPHBM4 standard to slash AI memory costs — Narrow 512-bit interface enables dropping expensive interposers for organic substrates
blurb: JEDEC released SPHBM4 (JESD330-4), a standard that pairs HBM4 DRAM stacks with a narrow 512-bit interface so memory can mount on inexpensive organic substrates instead of requiring interposers and advanced packaging like TSMC's CoWoS. To offset the narrower bus, the spec pushes data rates to 22.4-46.0 GT/s, aiming to lower the cost of high-bandwidth memory for AI processors.

title: Native-speed vLLM transformers modeling backend
blurb: Hugging Face upgraded the transformers modeling backend for vLLM, letting any supported Hugging Face model run inside vLLM with a single `--model-impl transformers` flag and no porting work. In head-to-head tests across Qwen3 4B dense, 32B tensor-parallel, and 235B FP8 Mixture-of-Experts models, the backend composes with existing tensor, data, and expert parallelism to approach the throughput of vLLM's hand-written native implementations.

title: Reason Less, Verify More: Deterministic Gates Recover a Silent Policy-Violation Failure Mode in Tool-Using LLM Agents
blurb: This paper studies "silent" policy violations in tool-using LLM agents, where a tool executes a forbidden state transition and neither the tool nor the agent's self-report flags it, accounting for 78% of failures in the τ²-bench airline domain. Adding deterministic, read-only gates that inspect each proposed call before a write raised full-benchmark success from 29.6% to 42.0% on gpt-4o-mini and from 61.2% to 71.6% on gpt-5.2.

title: Paradigm raises $1.2 billion fourth fund, expanding beyond crypto into AI and robotics
blurb: Paradigm raised a $1.2B fourth fund that will invest in crypto, AI, robotics, and other frontier technology startups, marking the crypto venture firm's expansion beyond digital assets. Its non-crypto bets already include AI research company Nous Research, drone delivery firm Zipline, and space defense startup True Anomaly, alongside crypto holdings like Hyperliquid and Kalshi.

title: The Blind Curator: How a Biased Judge Silently Disables Skill Retirement in Self-Evolving Agents
blurb: This paper shows that self-evolving agents lose their ability to retire bad skills when the LLM judge scoring their work is biased toward false passes, where failures slip through as passes. The authors find that false-pass bias silently disables contribution-based skill retirement past a sharp threshold, degrading no aggregate metric, and propose a cheap defect-injection audit that tells operators before deployment which side of that threshold their judge falls on.

title: GitHub Copilot Refuses Harmful Requests in Chat, Then Writes Them in Code
blurb: A new study of GitHub Copilot found that coding assistants which refuse harmful requests in chat will produce the same banned content when the request is broken into ordinary coding steps. Researchers Abhishek Kumar and Carsten Maple call the method workflow-level jailbreak construction: Copilot is asked to build a benign test program, then to add example answers, and it writes the dangerous text itself. Across four models, direct chat prompts succeeded 8 of 816 times, but the workflow framing produced harmful content in all 816 runs.

title: Malicious AI agent skills can slip past the scanners built to stop them
blurb: Researchers at the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology built SkillCloak, a system that preserves the malicious behavior of AI agent skills while rewriting their appearance to evade install-time scanners. Tested against a wide set of detectors on 1,613 real malicious skills, its file-packing technique slipped past every scanner more than 90% of the time.

title: OpenAI launches GPT-Live voice model series ahead of broad GPT-5.6 release
blurb: OpenAI introduced GPT-Live, a family of voice models with a full-duplex architecture that lets the system interrupt, search the web, or act mid-sentence instead of waiting for users to finish speaking. GPT-Live-1 will be the default in paid ChatGPT plans and GPT-Live-1-mini will power the free tier, with both routing complex prompts to GPT-5.5 and rolling out across iOS, Android, and web.

title: Single-Rollout Asynchronous Optimization for Agentic Reinforcement Learning
blurb: This paper introduces Single-rollout Asynchronous Optimization (SAO), a reinforcement learning method that replaces GRPO's group-wise sampling with one rollout per prompt to cut off-policy effects and stabilize asynchronous agentic training. SAO trains stably for a thousand steps, outperforms GRPO variants on SWE-Bench Verified, BeyondAIME, and IMOAnswerBench, and was deployed to train the open GLM-5.2 model (750B-A40B).

title: Rethinking Code Performance Benchmarks for LLMs
blurb: This paper re-evaluates four popular code-efficiency benchmarks for LLMs, EffiBench, Enamel, EvalPerf, and Mercury, and finds that only 6.11% of "performant" implementations are significantly faster than canonical solutions under 30-run statistical testing. The authors trace the gap to weak benchmark tests and propose a three-agent framework that generates, diagnoses, and repairs performance tests, exposing significant improvements in about 25% of previously flat tasks.

title: Beyond Attack-Success Rate: Action-Graded Severity Scale for Tool-Using AI Agents
blurb: This paper introduces an action-graded harm rubric that scores a tool-using agent's trajectory on a seven-level ordinal scale (L0 to L6), replacing the binary attack-success rate with a measure of how reversible, cross-scope, and privilege-expanding the executed action was. Across four victim models on AgentDojo, the scale exposed a defense reporting zero attack success while still leaking data cross-scope, and a three-judge panel reproduced the deterministic oracle at Krippendorff's alpha of 0.91.

title: MiniMax M3: How Sparse Attention Makes Long-Horizon Agents Practical
blurb: This post explains how MiniMax M3 uses sparse attention to make long-horizon agents practical, arguing that the real advantage shows up in sustained agentic workloads rather than headline benchmark comparisons against Opus. It details how sparse attention cuts the compute and memory cost of long contexts, letting agents maintain state across extended multi-step tasks without the quadratic scaling that limits dense-attention models.

title: Mitigating Taint-Style Vulnerabilities in MCP Servers via Security-Aware Tool Descriptions
blurb: This paper analyzes vulnerabilities in MCP servers and finds that taint-style flaws make up a large share, require heavy code changes to fix, and receive slow community responses. The authors propose SPELLSMITH, which builds tool-level risk profiles and embeds behavioral guidance into the protocol's Description property, using LLM self-reflection to mitigate exploitation without relying on context-specific code fixes.

title: Any-Dimensional Learning by Sampling
blurb: This paper presents a unified framework that uses random sampling maps to compare machine learning inputs of different sizes, such as point clouds, token sequences, and graphs. The approach yields explicit generalization and sketching rates for models like permutation-invariant transformers and graph neural networks, characterizing when sampling with replacement, random binning, or species sampling applies.

title: Think Big, Search Small: Where Capacity Matters in Hierarchical Search Agents?
blurb: This paper factorizes hierarchical LLM search agents into delegation, execution, and answer-generation roles, then sweeps model capacity along each axis across five multi-hop QA benchmarks. Scaling the delegation backbone lifts exact match by roughly 11 points while scaling the executor moves it only 2.6, and a 1.7B executor distilled from filtered trajectories matches a frontier sub-agent while using 37% fewer tokens.

title: Writer AI Flaw Could Let Agent Previews Leak Session Tokens Across Tenants
blurb: Sand Security researchers disclosed WriteOut, a now-patched critical vulnerability in enterprise AI platform Writer that let attackers hijack accounts across separate organizations with a single preview link. When a logged-in user clicked an attacker's shared agent preview, Writer's proxy forwarded their session cookie into the attacker-controlled sandbox, exposing private chats, documents, connectors, LLM credentials, and in some cases administrative control.

title: Reducing Doom Loops with Final Token Preference Optimization
blurb: Liquid AI introduced Antidoom, a training method that curbs "doom loops," the repetitive degeneration where a reasoning model repeats a span like "Wait, let me reconsider" until the context window fills. It uses Final Token Preference Optimization to retrain the model's preference at the single token that starts a loop, cutting loop rates on an LFM2.5-2.6B checkpoint from 10.2% to 1.4% while improving eval scores.

title: Data for Agents
blurb: NVIDIA Nemotron released a collection of open datasets aimed at agent development, spanning software engineering traces, tool-use failures, multi-step reasoning, retrieval, and workflow execution. The release leans heavily on synthetic data, which NVIDIA argues lets teams preserve useful training signals without exposing proprietary sources while making agent behavior more inspectable and reproducible.

title: [AINews] Lilian Weng summarizes 35 papers on Harness Engineering for RSI
blurb: Lilian Weng, now a Thinky cofounder, published a recap of 35 papers on harness engineering and its role in recursive self-improvement. The post lays out proven harness design trends and reviews the optimization literature from the ACE paper to newer approaches like Meta-Harnesses, arguing that even as harness gains get absorbed into core models, the need to specify goals and context remains.

title: Cybersecurity and the Gap Between Skill and Ability
blurb: Schneier argues that AI is widening the historical gap between skill and ability, letting attackers with minimal knowledge autonomously hack networks, steal data, and deploy ransomware with little direction. He points to Five Eyes agencies' recent joint warning and concludes that any real defense will require harnessing AI for security as much as attackers are using it for offense.

title: Nvidia touts Vera CPU's single-threaded performance as its agentic AI advantage, reveals next-gen 'Rigel' Arm CPU cores — frames chip as a 'max single-threaded CPU at scale,' not a parallel monster
blurb: Nvidia is positioning its 88-core Vera CPU as a "max single-threaded CPU at scale," arguing that AI inference and agentic workloads are bound by single-thread speed because each reasoning step depends on the previous one. The company also revealed its next-gen Rigel Arm v9.2 core, shipping in the Rosa CPU, which promises higher per-core performance than Vera's Olympus core within the same silicon footprint.

title: Cognition releases SWE-1.7, trained from Kimi K2.7 and available at 1,000 tokens/second, claiming it nears GPT-5.5 and Opus 4.8 on benchmarks at a lower cost (Cognition)
blurb: Cognition released SWE-1.7, a coding model distilled from Kimi K2.7 that runs at 1,000 tokens per second. The company claims it approaches GPT-5.5 and Opus 4.8 on benchmarks while costing significantly less to run.

title: Breaking Database Lock-in: Agentic Regeneration of High Performance Storage Readers for Database Bypass
blurb: Jailbreak is an approach that bypasses the database engine entirely by reading storage files directly and materializing data as in-memory Apache Arrow columnar buffers. It uses LLM code synthesis to regenerate format-specific readers from database source code and documentation, validating correctness against JDBC/ODBC baselines on TPC-H and reaching up to 27x faster analytical throughput on PostgreSQL and MySQL.

title: PALS: Percentile-Aware Layerwise Sparsity for LLM Pruning
blurb: PALS is a one-shot LLM pruning method that sets per-layer sparsity from the 99th percentile of activation magnitudes, bounded to ±5% around the target ratio and requiring no fine-tuning. On LLaMA-2-7B at 50% sparsity it reaches 10.96 WikiText-2 perplexity versus 12.92 for uniform Wanda, though the authors note gains are architecture-dependent, with Mistral-7B showing none.

title: MIRA: Multiplayer Interactive World Models Trained on Rocket League
blurb: MIRA is a world model that generates interactive, multiplayer Rocket League gameplay, letting several players act in the same neural-simulated environment in real time. The project extends single-agent world models to a shared setting where the model must stay consistent across each player's inputs and viewpoints simultaneously.

title: Show HN: Rowboat – Open-source, local-first alternative to Claude Desktop
blurb: Rowboat is an open-source, local-first desktop AI assistant that indexes a user's work into a knowledge graph stored as plain Markdown on their machine. It bundles work surfaces including an email client, notes, browser, code mode, and meeting note taker, connects to external tools via MCP, and runs on local models through Ollama or LM Studio or hosted models with your own API key.

title: RLVP: Penalize the Path, Reward the Outcome
blurb: This paper introduces RLVP, a reinforcement learning method for real-world agents that penalizes constraint-violating actions along the path while still rewarding only the final outcome. The approach hits high task success with near-zero violations where outcome-only training breaks rules on nearly every episode, and it comes with four design rules for effective penalties, including how to avoid the inaction trap that occurs when a penalty is used alone.

title: SpaceXAI launches Grok 4.5 model on Grok Build and APIs
blurb: SpaceXAI released Grok 4.5, a flagship model built for coding and agentic workflows that is now live in Grok Build, Cursor, and the SpaceXAI API console, with EU access expected in mid-July. The model ships with a 500,000-token context window and scored 64.7% on SWE Bench Pro, which SpaceXAI says it completes using roughly 4.2 times fewer tokens than Opus 4.8.

title: Behavior Leverage Imbalance in Multi-Teacher On-Policy Distillation
blurb: This paper shows that multi-teacher on-policy distillation for agentic models can push a student toward over-calling tools, a behavior shift invisible in aggregate losses because it stems from a few high-leverage tokens at structural positions like `<tool_call>` rather than from greater token exposure. The authors propose Soft Clamp, a per-token divergence calibration method that cut over-calling from 13.7% to 9.0% on APIGen-MT relative to vanilla GKD while matching its decision accuracy.

title: Spider 2.0-AIFunc: Extending Real-World Text-to-SQL to AI-Native SQL Workflows
blurb: Spider 2.0-AIFunc is a benchmark of 465 verified instances across 125 real-world Snowflake databases that tests whether models can generate AI-native SQL, which calls LLM functions for classification, sentiment analysis, extraction, and similarity search inside ordinary queries. The strongest proprietary models reach 67-70% execution accuracy versus 58.1% for the best open-source model, and minimal agents match or beat elaborate text-to-SQL frameworks that fail to transfer to this setting.

title: Develop Humanoid Robot Policies End-to-End with NVIDIA Isaac GR00T
blurb: NVIDIA introduced the Isaac GR00T Development Platform, an open source, modular pipeline that unifies humanoid robot data collection, simulation-based training, large-scale evaluation, and deployment into a single workflow. At its center is GR00T 1.7, the first Apache 2.0-licensed vision-language-action model for generalized humanoid skills, pretrained on roughly 32K hours of human demonstration data and 8K hours of simulated rollouts.

title: Lone Attacker Uses AI to Breach AWS Cloud Environment in 72 Hours
blurb: Sygnia published research describing how a single financially motivated attacker used agentic AI workflows to breach a large AWS environment in roughly 72 hours and extort the victim. The intrusion chained weaknesses across application services, source code repositories, CI/CD pipelines, and data stores, using AI to accelerate reconnaissance, tool development, and command structuring at a volume that would typically take weeks.

title: Bug in top AI coding agents shows that Unix-era security headaches never really die
blurb: Wiz disclosed GhostApproval, a symlink-based vulnerability pattern in at least six major AI coding agents, including Claude Code, Cursor, and Amazon Q Developer, that lets attackers escape the workspace sandbox and achieve remote code execution on developers' machines. Amazon, Cursor, and Google rated it critical or high-severity and patched it, while Augment and Windsurf have acknowledged the report but not yet shipped fixes.

title: SpaceXAI launches Grok 4.5, touts lower coding-task costs than AI rivals
blurb: SpaceXAI launched Grok 4.5, a coding and agentic model priced at $2 per million input tokens and $6 per million output tokens and pitched at enterprises trying to control AI-assisted development costs. On Artificial Analysis' Coding Agent Index, the model ran roughly level with GPT-5.5 in Codex at an estimated $2.49 per task, versus $5.07 for GPT-5.5 and $11.80 for Fable 5 in Claude Code.

title: Network boundary for AI agents using NGINX and OpenTelemetry
blurb: This post presents a network boundary for AI agents built from two existing cloud-native components: NGINX as the traffic control plane and OpenTelemetry as the audit plane. NGINX acts as both reverse and forward proxy with iptables rules dropping all other egress, making the boundary architectural rather than policy-based, while the OTEL module emits a span per request for correlation and audit in tools like Jaeger, Grafana, or a SIEM.

title: SpaceXAI releases Grok 4.5, which Elon describes as an ‘Opus-class model’
blurb: SpaceXAI released Grok 4.5, its first model since going public, priced at $2 per million input tokens and $6 per million output tokens with claimed twice-greater token efficiency than rival models. Elon Musk called it an "Opus-class model" roughly comparable to Anthropic's Opus 4.7 on internal benchmarks, but faster and cheaper, though released metrics land just short of best-in-class.

title: Hackers can use 9 of the most popular AI tools to assemble massive botnets
blurb: Researchers demonstrated HalluSquatting, a pull-based prompt injection that exploits AI coding assistants' tendency to hallucinate package and resource identifiers to assemble botnets, run large-scale DDoS attacks, and infect devices at scale. The attack registers the identifiers LLMs are most likely to invent, seeds them with reverse-shell instructions, and works against Cursor, Gemini CLI, GitHub Copilot, Windsurf, Cline, and four other popular tools.

title: China to Let AI Firms Buy Nvidia H200s, Information Says
blurb: China plans to let its top AI companies buy a limited quantity of Nvidia H200 chips, according to the Information, signaling an easing of restrictions on the sought-after US technology. The move marks a shift from Beijing's prior discouragement of domestic firms purchasing high-end Nvidia hardware, though the permitted volumes remain capped.

title: OpenAI to publicly release GPT-5.6, rolls out conversational AI models
blurb: OpenAI will publicly release its GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna models on Thursday, roughly two weeks after limiting the rollout to a small group of trusted partners at the U.S. government's request. The company also introduced GPT-Live, a new generation of voice models that listen and speak simultaneously, with GPT-Live-1 and GPT-Live-1 mini rolling out to ChatGPT users globally.

title: AWS centralizes access, spending, and governance for Claude
blurb: AWS released Claude apps gateway, a self-hosted control plane that gives organizations a single point of control over access, costs, and policies for Claude Code and Claude Desktop. It replaces per-developer cloud credentials with OIDC single sign-on and short-lived tokens, and centralizes identity, policy, telemetry, routing, and spend caps across Amazon Bedrock or Claude Platform on AWS.

title: OpenAI to release GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra and Luna on July 9
blurb: OpenAI confirmed that its GPT-5.6 model series, comprising Sol, Terra, and Luna, will roll out globally to the public starting Thursday, July 9. The release follows regulatory clearance from the U.S. Department of Commerce, clearing the way for a worldwide public launch across all three models.

title: General Intuition just raised $320M on a thesis that sounds absurd — that video game data, not real robot telemetry, will produce the GPT of embodied AI
blurb: General Intuition raised $320M at a $2.3B valuation to build a foundation model for embodied AI, trained on millions of hours of action-labelled video game data rather than real-world robot telemetry. In its lead demonstration, the model powered a quadrupedal robot using only a front camera after fine-tuning on just eight minutes of real-world robotics data.

title: RL Post-Training Builds Compositional Reasoning Strategies
blurb: This paper studies whether RL post-training composes primitive skills into new strategies or merely amplifies latent ones, using a fully observable rewrite-grammar environment where every generated step can be audited. RL solved held-out problems that the pretrained model rarely reached even under larger sampling budgets, discovering reusable sequential and parallel compositions, while rejection fine-tuning plateaued by producing many invalid shortcut rewrites rather than concentrating exploration on valid structure.

title: Guidance Breaks the Fitted Operator: A Terminal-Fitted Repair for Classifier-Free Guidance
blurb: Classifier-free guidance sharpens class-conditioning in diffusion samplers but oversaturates and destabilizes at high guidance, which this paper attributes to a solver artifact where the DDIM step is no longer fitted on the final low-sigma stretch. The authors propose a one-coefficient, zero-extra-cost fix that replaces CFG's guidance term, removing the divergent blow-up and winning 9/9 point-FID comparisons on CIFAR-10.

title: From Noisy Traces to Root Causes: Structural Trajectory Analysis and Causal Extraction for Agent Optimization
blurb: STRACE (Structural TRajectory Analysis and Causal Extraction) is a framework that builds high signal-to-noise optimization contexts for reflection-based agent tuning, mining batches of execution traces to filter redundant failures and running causal localization over a textual dependency graph to isolate the true root-cause step. On the VeruSAGE-Bench formal verification task, it raised success rates from 42.5% to 58.5%, a 1.4x improvement over human-expert-designed agents.

title: DeLS-Spec: Decoupled Long-Short Contexts for Parallel Speculative Drafting
blurb: DeLS-Spec is a speculative decoding method that pairs a frozen DFlash block-parallel drafter, treated as a long-context expert, with a lightweight local head that acts as a short-context expert. The local head trains independently with standard next-token prediction, avoiding costly joint training, and improves speedup and average acceptance length over DFlash on Qwen3 across math, code, and dialogue benchmarks.

title: TF-Engram: A Train-Free Engram with SSD-Backed Memory for Large Language Models
blurb: TF-Engram is a train-free memory system that builds phrase-specific semantic tables offline from external corpora and stores them across a GPU-DRAM-SSD hierarchy, using Early-Exit Guided Predictive Prefetching to hide external-memory latency during decoding. On Qwen3-0.6B it raised the average downstream score from 57.6 to 59.4, beating both the frozen backbone and a parameter-matched LoRA baseline while cutting GPU memory demand.

title: Experiences with local models for coding
blurb: This second memo from a Thoughtworks technologist details hands-on experiences running small models locally for agentic coding on an M3 Max (48GB) and M5 Pro (64GB), rather than just autocomplete. The author evaluates candidates through a viability funnel of RAM fit, response speed, tool-calling, functional correctness, sustained context, task complexity, and final code quality.

title: SpaceXAI’s newest AI model Grok 4.5 dramatically undercuts Anthropic and OpenAI on price
blurb: SpaceXAI released Grok 4.5, its first major model since going public, priced at $2 per million input tokens and $6 per million output tokens, well under Anthropic's Opus 4.7 at $5 and $25. Musk claims the model is roughly comparable to Opus 4.7 on performance while running faster and cheaper, citing twice the token efficiency of rival frontier labs.

title: MedPMC: A Systematic Framework for Scaling High-Fidelity Medical Multimodal Data for Foundation Models
blurb: MedPMC is an automated framework that curated 11 million medical image-text pairs from 6.1 million PubMed Central articles, with manual review finding 95.3% of images medically relevant versus 19.7% in a prior PMC-derived dataset. A MedPMC-trained CLIP-style model improved average zero-shot AUC by 7.1 points across 26 benchmarks using fewer than half as many pairs, and the framework, corpus, and pretrained models are publicly released.

title: Specification Grounding Drives Test Effectiveness for LLM Code
blurb: This paper isolates whether the gains from LLMs writing their own tests come from the tests existing or from grounding them in a specification. Holding the tester, budget, and repair loop fixed and changing a single prompt line to feed the spec as a checklist produced correct code 38 percentage points more often across three Claude tiers, while doubling the test budget barely helped and cut the false-alarm rate from 33% to 0%.

title: AgentTether: Graph-Guided Diagnosis and Runtime Intervention for Reliable LLM Agent Operation
blurb: AgentTether is a runtime repair framework for LLM agents that diagnoses failed tool-use trajectories by abstracting each run into a dependency-aware Critical Transition Graph, localizing failure-critical subtrajectories, and applying guided recovery without retraining the agent or modifying its environment. Across 261 tau-bench tasks, it repaired 59% of failed Qwen3.7-max runs and 65% of failed GPT-5.4 runs on the hardest Banking domain while reducing agent turns and total tokens.

title: Behavior Foundations for Quadruped Robots: ABot-C0 Technical Report
blurb: ABot-C0 is a generalist motion-control system for quadruped robots built on a 16,074-clip motion corpus assembled through conditional video-generation synthesis, motion capture, teleoperation, and human design. Its Flow-Matching policy is the first to show a quadruped motion-tracking scaling law, improving consistently with training scale and tracking unseen motions zero-shot, while a LiDAR-memory locomotion stack handles all-terrain traversal.

title: Agents Work Better with Conventional CLIs
blurb: Microsoft tested whether replacing CLI flags with a single `--json` payload helps AI agents, building a synthetic tool called podctl with deeply nested, multi-service deployment specs to measure the difference. The data showed agents perform better with conventional flag-based CLIs than with structured JSON input, suggesting teams should not rewrite their tools' interfaces to accommodate agents.

title: Running Low-Latency Analytical Workloads with GPU-Accelerated Presto on NVIDIA GB200 NVL72
blurb: NVIDIA published a walkthrough of running GPU-accelerated Presto, the open-source distributed SQL engine, on single-node DGX B200 and multinode GB200 NVL72 systems. The post benchmarks TPC-H queries using cuDF algorithms and NVLink 5.0 at 1,800 GB/s, showing GPU Presto on one 8-GPU DGX B200 node outperforming CPU Presto spread across 8-10 Intel Xeon nodes.

title: ByteDance debuts Seedream 5.0 Pro with advanced reasoning
blurb: ByteDance Seed released Seedream 5.0 Pro, a multimodal image model built for production design work rather than one-shot generation, with region-based editing via point, lasso, box, and sketch selection plus layer separation into editable text, subject, and background assets. It supports more than 10 languages with right-to-left layouts and is rolling out to developers through BytePlus ModelArk, which can produce 2 to 10 reference images per prompt.

title: Institutional Red-Teaming: Deployment Rules, Not Just Models, Causally Shape Multi-Agent AI Safety
blurb: This paper introduces institutional red-teaming, a method for testing multi-agent AI safety by holding agents and objectives fixed while varying a single deployment rule, then attributing behavior changes to that rule. Across IABench-CA's 33,924 games, changing only the consequence rule shifted mean fatality by 22 to 58 percentage points, and naming the loss bearer in the rule text drove targeted elimination from 22% to 81% at identical payoffs.

title: Reward-Adaptive Iterative Discovery: A Case Study on Automated Game Testing for NHL26
blurb: This paper introduces Reward-Adaptive Iterative Discovery (RAID), a reinforcement learning method that automatically finds behavioral exploits in the goalie AI of EA SPORTS NHL 26. RAID adds a diversity extension to standard RL algorithms so a population of scoring agents converges on multiple distinct strategies, surfacing six scoring exploits in a single experiment that matched those human playtesters found over hours of manual testing.

title: FedCVESA: Taking Away Training Data in Federated Learning via Correlation Value Encoding and Segmented Aggregation
blurb: This paper presents FedCVESA, a white-box attack in which a malicious federated learning server encodes target clients' private training data into carrier parameters using a Pearson-correlation regularizer. Segmented aggregation preserves those parameters through averaging, letting the server recover semantically meaningful training images from MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10 while maintaining acceptable main-task accuracy.

title: Operational Reframing and Approval-Framed Delegation in Multi-Agent LLM Safety
