For heavy AI users watching token costs eat their margins, a model that lands just short of the frontier at half the price resets the routing math on which workloads justify a premium model.

title: The FTC settles with John Deere over a 2025 right-to-repair lawsuit, requiring the company to provide farmers and shops with equipment and software for 10 years (Boone Ashworth/Wired)
blurb: The FTC settled its 2025 right-to-repair lawsuit with John Deere, requiring the company to give farmers and independent shops the same diagnostic tools, software, and parts its dealers get for the next 10 years. The settlement turns repair access from a goodwill gesture into a binding obligation for one of the largest equipment makers in the country. For any founder building hardware with locked-down service ecosystems, the regulatory direction is now clear: the right to repair is becoming table stakes, not a differentiator you can withhold.

title: XBOX Cuts; Bundling and the Internet Solvent; Transaction, Coordination, and Sunk Costs
blurb: Microsoft is cutting Xbox jobs as its Game Pass bet unravels, and the essay reads it as a bundling failure rather than a market one. A subscription bundle only works when it lowers transaction and coordination costs enough to change behavior, and Game Pass instead trained players to rent access without ever building the sunk-cost attachment that keeps them buying. The lesson for operators is that a bundle has to compound loyalty, not just discount it.

title: SpaceX's Grok 4.5 launches at half the price of rivals — here's why that could rattle Anthropic and OpenAI
blurb: SpaceX released Grok 4.5, its first model trained specifically for coding and agents, and it competes on economics rather than benchmark scores. Musk calls it roughly comparable to Opus 4.7 but much faster, priced at $2 per million input tokens and $6 per million output, less than half what Anthropic and OpenAI charge for their frontier tiers. Artificial Analysis ranked it only fourth on capability but measured it at $0.49 per completed task, nearly 90% cheaper than the models ahead of it. The fight for enterprise agent workloads is shifting from smartest model to cheapest completed task.

title: Crypto VC Paradigm launches $1.2 billion AI fund as it broadens beyond digital assets
blurb: Paradigm, one of crypto's largest venture investors, raised a $1.2B fund focused on AI and robotics, its first vehicle to broaden past digital assets. The fund has already backed drone delivery firm Zipline, valued at $7.6B, and space defense startup True Anomaly at $2.2B. The move marks a pattern among crypto-native firms following capital toward AI and defense, though Paradigm says it stays committed to crypto after raising dedicated blockchain funds in 2021 and 2024.

title: The $10B FDE Boom
blurb: AI labs and cloud giants have committed $9.75B to forward-deployed engineering in the past 12 months, turning a Palantir signature move into an industry default. That is one quarter of Accenture's annual labor cost, and it is splitting into three models: balance-sheet teams like Salesforce's 1,000 committed FDE roles, standalone entities like OpenAI's $4B Deployment Company, and partner funds like Google Cloud's $750M pool. The bet is that model capability is no longer the bottleneck, deployment is, and embedded engineers create an institutional switching cost no API call can replicate.

title: Apple commits $30 billion to Broadcom for U.S. chipmaking push
blurb: Apple committed more than $30 billion to Broadcom for U.S.-made custom silicon, its largest domestic manufacturing pledge to date and the biggest piece of its $600 billion, four-year U.S. investment plan. The deal covers over 15 billion American-made chips through 2031, including custom ASICs increasingly used for AI workloads, plus a $1.5 billion expansion of Broadcom's Fort Collins facility. The direction is clear: Apple is bringing more of its silicon supply chain onshore under political pressure, and its connectivity and AI components are the first to move.

title: Block agrees to pay $45M and offer live customer support for Cash App to settle claims by 46 US states that the company failed to protect users from fraud (Daniel Wiessner/Reuters)
blurb: Block agreed to pay $45M and add live customer support for Cash App to settle claims from 46 US states that it failed to protect users from fraud. The states found Cash App leaned on automated support while fraud complaints piled up, treating human help as a cost to avoid rather than a safeguard. For any founder building a consumer fintech or a support-light product, the settlement sets a floor: regulators now treat access to a live human as part of the compliance surface, not an optional feature.

title: PitchBook: US venture funding hits $412.7B in first half as AI deals dominate
blurb: US venture funding hit $412.7B in the first half of 2026, nearly 30% more than all of last year, and AI took $355.9B of that, 86% of every dollar deployed. The money keeps pooling at the top, with $100M-plus rounds accounting for 87.5% of the total while sub-$100M deals fell to a 12.5% slice, down from 43.8% two years ago. PitchBook calls the shift structural rather than cyclical, driven by AI coding tools and foundation models that let founders build on a base layer instead of training their own.

title: Transporter-17 Sends the First Commercial Nuclear Sat to Orbit
blurb: SpaceX's Transporter-17 rideshare carried the first commercial nuclear-powered payload to low Earth orbit, City Labs' BOHR satellite, which runs a tritium betavoltaic battery independent of solar arrays. The bigger unlock is regulatory, not technical: nuclear power has flown for decades under NASA, but City Labs won FAA authorization in September, establishing a pathway other commercial space nuclear missions can follow. The endgame is powering lunar payloads through the two-week lunar night and into permanently shadowed regions, where solar can't reach and most hardware simply dies.

title: Apple loses major antitrust appeal in Europe, remains a ‘gatekeeper’
blurb: Europe's General Court rejected all three of Apple's claims against its DMA "gatekeeper" designation, upholding the label for both iOS and the App Store and ruling its iMessage challenge inadmissible. The decision strengthens EU regulators pushing Apple to open its platform to competing app stores and payment options, and it removes Apple's last real hope of avoiding compliance short of an appeal to the Court of Justice. For founders building on iOS, the alternative-distribution and pricing terms the DMA forces open are now far more likely to stick than to be litigated away.

title: China Weighs Limits on the AI Models American Companies Love
blurb: Beijing is weighing new restrictions on the domestic AI models that many American companies quietly rely on, part of a broader move to tighten control over homegrown technology as the US-China AI race intensifies. Chinese open-weight models from labs like DeepSeek and Alibaba have become a cheap backbone for Western startups looking to cut inference costs. If access narrows or export rules harden, teams that built on those weights lose a supply line they may have assumed was permanent, and the cheapest path to frontier-adjacent performance gets political.

title: AI law startup Norm raises $120M, hits unicorn valuation
blurb: Norm raised $120M in a Series C led by Khosla Ventures, valuing the three-year-old legal AI startup at $1.2B. What sets it apart from rivals like Harvey and Legora is the model underneath: Norm runs its own AI-native law firm with human attorneys supervising the agents, and it charges on outcomes instead of billable hours. The bet is that legal work moves to fixed-price, agent-run delivery, and the investor list, which includes former leaders of Blackstone and Kirkland & Ellis, suggests the industry's own insiders think the hourly model is worth disrupting.

title: Agentic AI adoption is on fire at Uber
blurb: Agentic AI adoption is accelerating fast inside Uber, with the company moving from experiments to systems that plan and execute work across engineering, operations, and support. The signal for founders is that a large, process-heavy incumbent is treating agents as production infrastructure rather than a pilot, which resets the bar for what "AI adoption" means at scale. When a company this size wires agents into core workflows, the competitive pressure moves downstream to everyone selling into or against it.

title: Bezos’ Space Venture Blue Origin Seeks $10 Billion in Funding Round
blurb: Blue Origin is raising $10B at a $130B valuation, its first round from outside investors after two decades funded almost entirely by Jeff Bezos. The raise signals that private space is shifting from founder-financed moonshot to institutionally backed infrastructure, with capital pooling behind a small set of players competing with SpaceX. For founders, it marks how far the bar has moved in capital-intensive hard tech, where the winners now command valuations and war chests that make late entry nearly impossible.

title: 하버드·인시아드, 스타트업 2,891곳 비교했더니··· “AI 네이티브 기업, 인력 25% 적고 직급 계층도 더 단순”
blurb: A new INSEAD and Harvard study of 2,891 YC startups found that AI-native companies run about 25% smaller headcount and roughly half a layer flatter than same-vintage peers, yet raise similar capital at comparable valuations. That leaves value per employee 30% to 76% higher. The structural shift comes from the product channel, not the process channel: firms that build AI into the product itself run leaner, while merely handing staff tools like Cursor or Copilot changes nothing. In service-heavy sectors like tutoring and healthcare, the AI startups carried around 70% fewer employees.

title: Bezos' Blue Origin valued at $130 billion in first outside fundraising round
blurb: Blue Origin is raising about $10 billion in its first outside round, valuing Jeff Bezos' rocket company at $130 billion, with Bezos putting in $2 billion and Coatue another $4 billion. The round lands weeks after SpaceX's record $86 billion IPO pushed Musk's company to a roughly $2 trillion valuation. Bezos, who until now funded Blue Origin entirely through Amazon stock sales, is opening the cap table just as the two compete head-to-head on heavy-lift rockets, lunar landers, and satellite internet, where Musk holds a wide lead.

title: Lovable reportedly in talks to double its valuation to $13.2B
blurb: Lovable is in talks to raise $300M at a $13.2B valuation, exactly double the $6.6B it hit last December, with Menlo Ventures expected to lead. The less-than-three-year-old Swedish startup crossed $500M in annualized revenue run rate in June, and it sells its vibe-coding tool to enterprises like Workday, Asana, and Nvidia. Vibe coding is now the most lucrative use case for AI, with Replit at $9B, Factory at $1.5B, and Cursor acquired by SpaceX for $60B last month, so the category is repricing every few weeks.

title: Blue Origin, for the first time, is expected to raise private capital
blurb: Blue Origin is raising $10B at a $130B valuation, its first outside capital in 26 years, with Coatue leading a $4B commitment and Bezos adding $2B. Until now the company ran almost entirely on Bezos's money, roughly several billion a year, while SpaceX built its war chest from contracts, loans, and an IPO that pulled in $85B at a $2 trillion valuation. The raise is as much about talent as hardware, since Blue Origin needs its own equity story to compete with the stock options SpaceX dangles in front of engineers.

title: Prime Intellect raises $130M Series A to help enterprises build their own AI agents
blurb: Prime Intellect raised $130M at a $1B valuation to sell enterprises a full stack for building their own AI agents, letting them train specialized systems without renting a frontier lab. The bet is that reinforcement learning now lets a company become its own AI lab, and the infrastructure is complex enough that most will pay for compute, an RL framework, and evals bundled together. Adoption from Ramp, Zapier, and others has pushed the startup to a $100M revenue run rate, with Ramp reporting its in-house agent beat frontier models on accuracy at a fraction of the cost.

title: Shanghai-based GPU maker Iluvatar CoreX raised ~$902M in a Hong Kong share sale; the company's stock has soared 257% since its January IPO, which raised $473M (Bloomberg)
blurb: Chinese GPU maker Iluvatar CoreX raised about $902M in a Hong Kong share sale, and its stock has climbed 257% since a January IPO that pulled in $473M. The demand reflects a scramble to fund domestic alternatives to Nvidia as export controls keep the best US chips out of China. For founders, the signal is that capital is flowing hard into homegrown AI infrastructure, and a parallel compute stack is being built with real money behind it, not just policy statements.

title: Sources: Reno-based AI chip startup Positron is in talks to raise ~$750M in two phases, at valuations of $3.5B in the first tranche and ~$5B in the second (Bloomberg)
blurb: Positron, a Reno-based AI chip startup, is in talks to raise roughly $750M across two phases, at a $3.5B valuation on the first tranche and about $5B on the second. The staged structure lets it lock capital now while pricing the second slug against milestones it hasn't hit yet. The raise signals that investors are still willing to fund challengers to Nvidia's inference dominance at multi-billion valuations, even as the compute capex required to compete keeps climbing.

title: Wall Street Banks Miss Multibillion-Dollar AI Deals in Hong Kong
blurb: Chinese AI startups raised $5.8B in Hong Kong this week, and Wall Street's biggest banks were largely shut out of the deals. The absence signals how much of the AI financing engine is now running through Asian markets and local underwriters rather than the traditional New York bulge-bracket firms. For founders raising in the region, the capital and the dealmaking relationships increasingly sit closer to home, and the roster of who can actually get a mega-round done is shifting fast.

title: China Chipmaker CXMT to Launch $4.3 Billion IPO Next Week
blurb: China's largest memory chipmaker, CXMT, will open subscriptions next week for a $4.3B IPO, the country's most anticipated listing of the year. The raise gives a state-backed DRAM producer fresh capital to scale against Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron while US export controls keep tightening. For founders building on memory-hungry AI hardware, it signals that China is funding a domestic supply chain meant to survive being cut off from Western chips.

title: Zhipu Surges 22% After $4 Billion Share Sale Priced at Low End
blurb: Zhipu raised $4 billion in a Hong Kong share sale priced at the low end of its range, yet the stock still surged as much as 22% on Thursday. The gap says investors are willing to fund China's frontier model makers even when the placement itself signals caution. For founders, it marks how much capital is now flowing to non-US labs, widening the field of well-funded competitors building foundation models outside the OpenAI and Anthropic orbit.

title: Nvidia Rival Positron Holds Talks to Raise Funds at $5 Billion Valuation
blurb: AI chip startup Positron is in talks to raise about $750 million in a two-phase round that would value it at $5 billion, joining a wave of companies now funded to challenge Nvidia's dominance. The money is chasing inference-specific silicon, where the bet is that Nvidia's general-purpose GPUs leave room for cheaper, purpose-built chips. Capital is flowing to the challengers faster than any of them has shipped volume, which tells you investors are pricing the AI hardware market as a category too large for one supplier to keep.

title: SpaceX stock closes below debut price at $148 in two-day slide after Nasdaq-100 inclusion
blurb: SpaceX closed at $148 on Wednesday, below its $150 debut price for a second straight day, less than a month after its June 12 listing and one day after joining the Nasdaq-100. The index inclusion forced tied ETFs to buy shares, yet the stock still slid from its $201.80 high. Analysts stayed mostly bullish, with Morgan Stanley, Bernstein, RBC, and UBS setting targets from $210 to $300 on Starlink margins, reusable-launch dominance, and a pitch to build AI products and orbital data centers.

title: SambaNova hits $11 billion valuation as investors back Nvidia chip challengers
blurb: SambaNova raised $1B at an $11B valuation, led by General Atlantic, as investors keep funding startups aiming at Nvidia's lead. The bet is on inference rather than training: SambaNova's SN50 server units target fast, cost-efficient model serving, with a focus on on-premise deployments that keep private data inside a company's firewall. JPMorgan just signed on as the bank's inference provider, and CEO Rodrigo Liang is weighing a US IPO in 2027. The money is moving toward whoever can run agents cheaply, not just train the largest models.

title: Inference chip startup SambaNova valued at $11B in $1B funding round
blurb: SambaNova raised $1B at an $11B valuation in a Series F led by General Atlantic, with Intel Capital, Vista Equity, and JPMorgan among the participants. The round bets on inference, not training, where the company's new SN50 chip claims more than three times the throughput of Nvidia's B200 by putting SRAM next to the processing circuits and cutting the memory round-trips that slow prompt responses. The money chasing Nvidia alternatives is now concentrating on inference economics, the part of the AI stack where cost per token, not raw training power, decides who wins.

title: Starbucks Taps AI to Cut Reliance on Microsoft, IBM Software
blurb: Starbucks is building in-house tools with AI that could replace software it currently buys from vendors like Microsoft and IBM. The shift matters because it turns a large enterprise buyer into a builder, and if AI makes custom software cheap enough to own rather than license, incumbent vendors lose the switching costs that protect their renewals. For founders selling into the enterprise, the question is no longer just which competitor might win the account, but whether the customer decides to build the thing themselves.

title: Former GitHub CEO’s startup Entire unveils its answer to the crush of AI coding agents
blurb: Former GitHub CEO Thomas Dohmke's startup Entire launched a preview of its distributed Git network, letting developers mirror an existing GitHub repo in one step so AI agents clone and pull from a faster, closer copy. The bet is that centralized hosting buckles when billions of agents hammer a single server, showing up as rate limits, latency, and outages. Entire emerged in February with a $60M seed led by Felicis at a $300M valuation, and it's positioning as complementary to Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex rather than a GitHub competitor, with Microsoft's M12 among the backers.

title: VC firm Chemistry is raising $500M for its second fund
blurb: Chemistry Ventures is raising $500M for its second fund, two years after launching with $350M, according to an SEC filing. The firm, started by alums of Bessemer, Index, and a16z, backs early-stage AI startups and already counts Granola, Decagon, and Persona in its portfolio. The Wall Street Journal reports the fund is oversubscribed and closing soon, a sign that capital keeps concentrating with new AI-focused managers even as the broader venture backlog grows.

title: Payloads used to dictate the terms of launch. That's finally changing.
blurb: For decades, rocket designers built their vehicles to match whatever the satellite industry was already ordering, and payloads dictated the terms. Starship inverts that logic, offering more than 100 metric tons to low-Earth orbit and, with refueling, the same mass to the Moon or Mars. Now NASA, the US military, telescope builders, and satellite manufacturers are redesigning around the capacity rather than asking the rocket to shrink to fit them. The vehicle is still experimental and unproven on Musk's biggest claims, but abundant super-heavy-lift is already reshaping what operators think they can build.

title: What a Founder Who Sold His Last Company for $780M Is Unlearning
blurb: Zain Jaffer sold Vungle to Blackstone for $780M running the classic SaaS playbook, and he now spends Blazel's all-hands meetings unlearning it. The org chart is the first casualty, since middle-management layers that signaled scale in 2018 now suffocate the speed AI-native execution demands. Defensibility has also moved: the model is rented from whichever lab is cheapest this quarter, so the only durable moats are owned audience, brand, and a proprietary dataset the labs can't replicate without your customers.

title: Despite ‘misgivings,’ judge approves Elon Musk’s $1.5M SEC settlement
blurb: A federal judge approved Elon Musk's $1.5M SEC settlement over his 2022 Twitter stake disclosure, while writing that she had "significant misgivings" about it. The SEC argued Musk's delayed disclosure saved him roughly $150M, so the penalty settles the case at a fraction of what the late filing was worth, with no admission of wrongdoing. Judge Sooknanan had earlier questioned whether Musk got "special treatment" from an administration he helped bankroll, but ruled she could only block a deal that made "a mockery of judicial power.

title: Companies are mobilizing internal groups of "AI champions" to drive adoption; BCG says 74% of front-line employees now use AI regularly, up from 51% in 2025 (Isabelle Bousquette/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Companies are now spinning up internal "AI champion" programs to push adoption, and the usage curve backs the effort. BCG reports 74% of front-line employees use AI regularly, up from 51% a year ago. The lever isn't the tooling anymore, it's the internal advocates who make the tools stick, which means adoption is becoming an org-design problem more than a procurement one.

title: Filings: Grubhub parent Wonder is raising hundreds of millions of dollars at a $9B valuation; a source says founder Marc Lore indicated he will contribute $200M (The Information)
blurb: Grubhub parent Wonder is raising hundreds of millions of dollars at a $9B valuation, with founder Marc Lore indicated to chip in $200M of his own money, according to filings and a source. The round layers more capital onto a company still stitching together food delivery, meal kits, and its earlier Grubhub acquisition into a single ordering platform. Lore's personal contribution signals conviction that the all-in-one food model can scale, even as delivery economics stay thin and the path to profitability remains the open question.

title: Muse Image, Grok 4.5, Alex Karp on CNBC
blurb: Verifiable data is becoming the axis the AI race turns on, with Meta's Muse Image, xAI's Grok 4.5, and the frontier labs all organizing around it. The advantage no longer comes from raw model scale but from owning data whose correctness can be checked and rewarded. The labs that lock up verifiable domains, from code to math to structured records, set the terms everyone else builds on.

title: AI Capex Cycle Hasn't Reached Overspend Yet: BlackRock
blurb: BlackRock says the AI capex cycle hasn't hit overspend yet, with fundamental equities CIO Helen Jewell arguing the next few years are clear because of how much capital will keep flowing into the space. Every capex cycle eventually reaches the point where overspend starts, but she doesn't believe the market is there now. For founders, the read is that the money backing AI infrastructure isn't slowing, so the constraint on building isn't capital availability but whether the returns show up before that cycle turns.

title: Meta is building its first big Canadian data center as AI expansion crosses the border
blurb: Meta is spending about $9 billion on a 1 gigawatt data center in Alberta, its first in Canada and 33rd overall, drawn north by the province's cheap energy and light regulation. The buildout is part of a capex plan that reaches up to $145 billion this year, a number investors keep pushing back on because Meta trails OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google on models and still has no revenue path beyond ads. The tell is that Meta is now planning a cloud business to sell excess capacity, chasing the hyperscaler playbook from behind.

title: China’s Zhipu AI Shares Surge on $4 Billion Fundraising
blurb: Zhipu AI's shares surged after the Chinese AI firm announced a $4B fundraising to fund its growth, even though it priced the new shares at a discount. The discount pricing alongside a jumping stock signals investors are willing to absorb dilution to keep Chinese frontier labs capitalized against US-backed rivals. For founders, it is another sign that the AI capital race is now a two-country contest, with Beijing's champions raising at a scale that keeps them in the frontier-model game rather than falling behind on compute.

title: Blue Origin seeks to raise $10B from external investors in first VC-led funding round
blurb: Blue Origin is in talks to raise $10B from outside investors at a valuation above $130B, its first VC-led round after 25 years of Bezos self-funding. Coatue would lead with $4B, Bezos would add $2B, and large institutions cover the rest, a round that dwarfs the previous pure-play space record of Stoke Space's $860M. The valuation would top Lockheed Martin, but it still leaves Blue Origin an order of magnitude behind SpaceX, which cleared a $1T market cap after its recent IPO. Even Bezos now needs outside capital to keep pace in space.

title: Quantum startup Oratomic banks $300M to race straight to fault-tolerance
blurb: Oratomic raised $300M in a Series A co-led by ARCH Venture Partners, Spark Capital, and Khosla Ventures, less than four months after launching. The neutral-atom startup, built with Caltech, is betting it can reach a cryptographically relevant fault-tolerant machine with roughly 10,000 reconfigurable qubits, far below the million-qubit targets rivals like IQM are chasing. The wager is that better error-correction and hardware quality beat brute-force scale, and investors are pricing an unproven early-stage team as if that bet is already close to paying off.

title: Should You Discount to Save a Renewal? The NRR Trap Says No. You Probably Should Anyway.
blurb: Holding the line on renewal pricing to protect NRR is the wrong instinct for a lot of legacy vendors right now, because the customers shopping around often don't want to leave, they just stopped seeing the price match the value. A big discount doesn't only save the logo, it buys another year to re-earn the business and fix what's broken. Reps hate discounting into renewals and finance hates the ASP hit, but letting a savable account walk over a few points of blended NRR is the costlier trade.

title: Samsung chip division's single-year profits beat its past 40 years of profits, combined, due to increased memory and storage prices — Samsung passes Nvidia to become most profitable company in the world, notches 19x quarterly increase in profit
blurb: Samsung's chip division expects its 2026 operating profit to exceed everything it has earned in roughly 40 years in the memory business, combined. A 19x quarterly jump in operating profit, to $58.5 billion, pushed Samsung past Nvidia's $53.54 billion to become the most profitable company in the world last quarter. The driver is AI server demand outrunning DRAM and NAND supply, lifting memory margins toward 40% to 50% and putting the swing in the AI buildout with the chipmakers selling the memory, not just the accelerators.

title: Munich’s QuantumDiamonds raises €91 million to scale its quantum-based semiconductor inspection technology
blurb: QuantumDiamonds, a TU Munich spinout, raised €91M to scale quantum-based semiconductor inspection, combining a €15M equity round led by World Fund with €76M in non-dilutive EU Chips Act funding from Germany and Bavaria. Its diamond-based sensor works as a microscope for electric currents, catching buried defects in 3D chip architectures that today's tools miss. The pitch lands on yield economics: a single percentage point of yield can be worth millions a week on a high-volume device, and QD is already deployed in the US and Taiwan with serial production ramping in Munich.

title: Europe Posted Its Strongest Venture Funding Quarter In 4 Years As UK Gains, M&A Holds Up
blurb: Europe posted its strongest venture quarter in four years, with startups raising $24B in Q2, up around a third quarter over quarter and two-thirds above the $14.4B of Q2 2025. Four billion-dollar rounds drove a quarter of that, but the real engine was 42 companies raising $100M-plus across biotech, quantum, robotics, and AI labs. The UK took more than $10B for its third-largest quarter on record, yet Europe's $42B H1 still trails North America's $392B, where funding grew 158%. The gap is narrowing at the top while the base stays thin.

title: Chip testing startup QuantumDiamonds raises €15M in equity funding
blurb: QuantumDiamonds, a Technical University of Munich spinout that uses quantum sensing to find defects in semiconductors, raised €15M in equity led by climatetech investor World Fund, bringing its total funding to €91M once €76M in non-dilutive German and Bavarian state aid is counted. The round funds a Munich testing facility that Europe is positioning as core to its bid to build chips without leaning on US tech. With deployments live in the US and Taiwan and headcount at 70, the company plans to more than double its engineering team over the next year.

title: Europe’s biggest new funds in H1 2026
blurb: Europe's biggest new funds in H1 2026 are landing despite a tougher fundraising landscape, with cautious LPs, slower exits, and fewer distributions squeezing the managers trying to raise. The capital that is closing skews toward scaleups, AI, and climate, and a large share of it now comes from institutional anchors like the European Investment Fund rather than a broad LP base. The pattern points to a barbell market, where a handful of established managers still clear billion-euro targets while everyone else fights for a shrinking pool.

title: Jeff Bezos just opened Blue Origin to outside investors for the first time in 26 years. Here’s what changed his mind
blurb: Blue Origin is raising roughly $10B from outside investors at a $130B pre-money valuation, the first outside capital Jeff Bezos has taken in the 26 years since founding the company. For decades he self-funded it by selling Amazon stock, but the cost of competing for lunar, defense, and commercial launch contracts has outgrown even one of the world's richest people. Bezos is putting in about $2B himself and Coatue roughly $4B, yet SpaceX, fresh off the largest IPO in history at a ~$2T valuation, is still worth about 15 times more.

title: QuantumDiamonds raises €91M, becomes the first startup approved for manufacturing funding under the EU Chips Act
blurb: QuantumDiamonds raised €91M and became the first startup to win manufacturing funding under the EU Chips Act, a program otherwise reserved for giants like GlobalFoundries and STMicroelectronics. The round splits into €15M equity led by World Fund and €76M non-dilutive from German and Bavarian governments. The bet is yield: the Munich company's diamond-based inspection sees defects that optical and X-ray tools miss in advanced 3D packages, and it has already tested with nine of the ten largest chipmakers. Europe makes 10% of the world's chips while consuming 20%, which is the math driving the approval.

title: TeraWulf shares surge on $19B Anthropic AI infrastructure lease deal
blurb: TeraWulf shares jumped 13% after signing a 20-year lease with Anthropic for AI infrastructure capacity, expected to generate about $19B in contracted revenue backed by investment-grade credit. The deal covers 401 MW at its Kentucky campus, with first capacity online in late 2027 and full buildout by early 2028. TeraWulf is also selling its 50.1% stake in a Texas joint venture to a Fluidstack-led group at roughly $450M, recycling that capital into wholly owned projects where it keeps full control.

title: Companies hire more after AI adoption
blurb: Ramp published its first working paper joining firm-level spend data with Revelio workforce data across more than 21,000 US firms, and it found companies grow headcount 10.2% in the two years after adopting AI. The gains are entirely driven by high-intensity adopters spending on coding agents and APIs, not chat subscriptions, at about $30 per employee per month. Entry-level headcount at the biggest AI investors grew 12%, so the roles AI was supposed to erase are the ones expanding fastest.

title: Samsung-backed AI chip firm Rebellions targets IPO in South Korea next year, CEO tells CNBC
blurb: Rebellions, the Samsung- and SK Hynix-backed AI inference chip startup, is targeting a KOSPI IPO in the first or second quarter of next year, with J.P. Morgan and Samsung Securities underwriting. The CEO cited real revenue and alignment with a Korean government AI infrastructure megaproject, though the company is also in talks with the NYSE and Nasdaq. The listing rides a wave of inference-chip interest, as buyers seek alternatives to Nvidia GPUs that run models faster and more efficiently. Cerebras already went public in the US this year, and the semiconductor index is up around 80%.

title: Venus Aerospace Raises $91M Series B to Scale RDRE Production
blurb: Venus Aerospace raised $91M in a Series B led by Mercury Fund, with Lockheed Martin Ventures and Draper Associates joining, to scale production of its rotating detonation rocket engine. The round follows Venus becoming the first US company to fly an RDRE-powered rocket in May 2025, an engine it claims is 15% more efficient than conventional designs and able to serve defense, in-space, and eventually high-speed aviation missions. RDRE is moving from academic concept to flight-proven hardware fast, with Stellar Alpina, Juno Propulsion, and JAXA all now building their own versions.

title: Venus Aerospace raises $91 million to scale rocket engine technology
blurb: Venus Aerospace raised $91M in a Series B led by Mercury Fund, with Lockheed Martin Ventures, Draper Associates, and PEAK6 joining, to scale its rotating detonation rocket engine from flight testing toward production. The RDRE generates thrust from a continuously rotating detonation wave instead of conventional combustion, a design that promises higher efficiency across hypersonic vehicles, munitions, space launch, and lunar landers. The round follows the first U.S. flight test of a 2,000-pound-thrust RDRE in May, and the defense-heavy cap table signals where the Pentagon's next-generation propulsion money is flowing.

title: Pixel-Flo lands £5.25M seed round for MicroLED manufacturing
blurb: Pixel-Flo, a University of Sheffield spin-out, raised £5.25M in seed funding led by Northern Gritstone to scale up its MicroLED manufacturing process. MicroLED beats conventional displays on brightness and energy efficiency, but cost and scalability have kept it out of mainstream products. Pixel-Flo's Continuous-Flow Mass Transfer process uses fluidic self-assembly to raise throughput while cutting processing costs and material use, and the round funds its move from lab to industrial scale-up.

title: How tech workers are feeling in 2026: a workforce splitting in two
blurb: Tech workers are splitting into two groups: those amplified by AI who feel more capable than ever, and those shaken by it who doubt their place. Which side someone lands on predicts their career sentiment more than title, tenure, or company size. Burnout jumped 11 points to 55.7% in a single year, and 53% would steer a newcomer away from their own role even while staying optimistic about their own future.

title: Dear SaaStr: What Happens if One Founder Wants to Quit When the Startup Has Just Raised VC Funding?
blurb: A co-founder walking away right after a raise is more common than founders expect, and the first move is telling your investors immediately rather than hiding it. From there you have two real options: recruit a senior late co-founder who can carry the leadership weight, or return what's left of the capital if the company genuinely can't survive without the person leaving. Owning the bad co-founder pick is part of the job, and with a few million in the bank, the stronger play is replacing one mistake with two or three better people.

title: Fleek raises £18.7m for its AI-native secondhand fashion marketplace
blurb: Fleek raised $25M (£18.7m) in Series B funding, led by early Vinted backer Burda Principal Investments, with eBay, FJ Labs, and existing investors Andreessen Horowitz and Y Combinator joining. The bet is on infrastructure for secondhand fashion, a market growing three times faster than traditional apparel but still run on manual, offline sorting. At the center is Fleek Sort, a vision-language model trained on millions of transactions that grades and lists garments from photos, digitizing a process that until now happened by hand in warehouses from Karachi to Dubai.

title: Warsaw VC Expeditions closes oversubscribed €197M Fund II
blurb: Warsaw-based VC Expeditions closed an oversubscribed €197M Fund II backed by BAE Systems, the European Investment Fund, and the NATO Innovation Fund. The vehicle targets early-stage defence, AI, cybersecurity, quantum, and space startups across Europe. The LP roster signals where European capital is now flowing, as sovereignty and defence tech pull institutional money into deep-tech categories that generalist funds have historically underweighted.

title: The Task Economy - Data will be the next $1 Trillion Category
blurb: Data becomes the next trillion-dollar category as AI shifts from inference tokens to the tasks those tokens complete.

title: A Startup That Builds AI Agents Used One to Raise $100 Million
blurb: A startup that builds AI agents for enterprises deployed one of its own during a funding round and raised $100 million. The pitch doubled as the product demo, turning the raise itself into a live proof of value that a slide deck never delivers. For founders selling agentic tools, the signal is clear: the strongest case study is running your own agent inside the workflow you're asking customers to trust it with.

title: Maritime defence startup Kraken Technology hits unicorn status
blurb: Kraken Technology raised $175M at a $1B valuation in a Series B led by DTCP, becoming Europe's latest defence unicorn just five years after its 2020 founding. The company builds uncrewed subsurface and surface vessels already deployed by NATO, the UK Ministry of Defence, and the US Navy, with Anduril as a partner and Rheinmetall and the NATO Innovation Fund among its backers. The round underscores how autonomous maritime hardware has become a fundable category, drawing strategic defence primes and sovereign capital into what used to be a purely venture bet.

title: Primo Capital targets €120M for its second space fund after its portfolio returned 8x
blurb: Primo Capital is raising €120M for Primo Space 2, its second fund backing civil and defence space startups, after its first fund returned more than 8x. The 19 companies from fund one have raised over €470M against roughly €55M invested, generated €115M in revenue, and created 600 jobs. McKinsey pegs the global space economy at $1.8T by 2035, but Europe still lacks specialist investors, and geopolitical pressure is pulling capital toward dual-use technology where space sits at the center.

title: Gemma 4 Technical Report
blurb: Google introduced Gemma 4, an open-weight multimodal family (2.3B–31B) adding thinking mode, MoE variants, and encoder-free audio/image ingestion.

title: Rewriting Bun in Rust
blurb: Bun's creator is rewriting the runtime from Zig to Rust, using a pre-release Claude Fable 5 for much of the port after Anthropic acquired Bun in December 2025. The post frames the switch around stability at Bun's massive scope, walking through a sample of v1.3.14 fixes for use-after-free and out-of-bounds bugs in node:zlib, node:http2, UDPSocket, and Buffer that Rust's memory model is meant to prevent.

title: GitHub 'Verified' Commits Can Be Rewritten Into New Hashes Without Breaking Signatures
blurb: New research shows that a signed Git commit's hash is malleable: without the signing key, an attacker can mint a second commit with identical files, author, and date, a valid signature, and GitHub's "Verified" badge, but a different hash. That breaks any system treating a verified hash as a permanent unique name, so hash blocklists, provenance logs, and reproducible-build records that key on the commit hash can all be bypassed.

title: TypeScript 7
blurb: Microsoft released TypeScript 7, a native port of the compiler rewritten in Go that runs 8x to 12x faster on full builds. The rewrite adds native code speed, shared-memory multithreading, and a new language server protocol implementation, so tsc, find-all-references, and editor autocompletions all run an order of magnitude faster in editors like VS Code and WebStorm.

title: AI Coding Tools Tricked Into Hacking Developer Machine via Decades-Old Technique
blurb: Wiz researchers demonstrated GhostApproval, an attack that plants a symbolic link in a benign-looking repository so that AI coding assistants resolve and write to sensitive files outside the workspace, potentially reaching remote code execution on the developer's machine. The core failure is that confirmation dialogs in tools like Claude Code, Cursor, Amazon Q Developer, and Windsurf display the symlink path rather than the canonical target, turning human-in-the-loop approval into a rubber stamp.

title: Postgres rewritten in Rust, now passing 100% of the Postgres regression tests
blurb: pgrust reimplements Postgres in Rust and passes 100% of Postgres's regression suite, matching expected output across more than 46,000 queries against Postgres 18.3. It is disk compatible and can boot from an existing 18.3 data directory, but it isn't production-ready or performance-optimized yet, and extensions like PL/Python and PL/Perl aren't generally supported.

title: Separating signal from noise in coding evaluations
blurb: OpenAI audited SWE-Bench Pro, the coding benchmark it had previously recommended over SWE-bench Verified, and estimates that roughly 30% of the 731 public tasks are broken. The flaws cluster into overly strict tests that reject functionally correct code, underspecified or misleading prompts, and low-coverage tests that pass incomplete fixes, undercutting a benchmark where frontier pass rates jumped from 23.3% to 80.3% in eight months.

title: SWE-1.7 Reach Near GPT 5.5 and Opus Intelligence
blurb: Cognition released SWE-1.7, a coding model trained from a Kimi K2.7 base that reaches near GPT-5.5 and Opus intelligence at a fraction of the cost. It is optimized for long-horizon asynchronous tasks using self-compaction to extend beyond the raw context window, and ships today in Devin (Web, Desktop, and CLI) via Cerebras at 1000 TPS.

title: Cloudflare Drop
blurb: Cloudflare Drop deploys a static site from a dragged-in folder or zip file, serving it across Cloudflare's global network within seconds with no build config, CLI, or account setup. It targets the same throwaway-deploy niche as Netlify Drop and Surge, trading pipeline control for the shortest possible path from local files to a live URL on Cloudflare's edge.

title: Revving up Microsoft’s 10x faster TypeScript 7
blurb: Microsoft shipped a release candidate for TypeScript 7, a native compiler port from JavaScript to Go that targets a 10x type-checking speedup by parallelizing work across independent checker and builder workers. It installs from npm alongside a preview VS Code language server, and includes breaking language and config changes from TypeScript 5 that require reading the migration docs before upgrading large codebases.

title: Show HN: Microsoft releases Flint, a visualization language for AI agents
blurb: Microsoft released Flint, an intermediate visualization language that compiles compact, human-editable chart specs into native Vega-Lite, ECharts, and Chart.js output. It infers scales, axes, spacing, and labels from the data and 70+ semantic field types like Price, Temperature, and Rank, and ships as both a `flint-chart` npm package and an MCP server so agents can generate charts across 30+ chart types from one backend-agnostic input.

title: OpenAI says it found widespread task issues in SWE-Bench Pro, estimates ~30% of tasks are broken, and retracts its earlier recommendation to adopt the benchmark (OpenAI)
blurb: OpenAI retracted its recommendation to adopt SWE-Bench Pro after finding roughly 30% of the benchmark's tasks are broken.

title: Astro 7.0
blurb: Astro 7 rewrites the .astro compiler, Markdown/MDX pipeline, and bundler path in Rust, and pairs with Vite 8's new Rolldown bundler to cut build times 15-61% in the team's benchmarks. The release also stabilizes route caching, adds experimental CDN cache providers for Netlify, Vercel, and Cloudflare, and ships an Advanced Routing entrypoint at src/fetch.ts for full control over the request pipeline.

title: New HalluSquatting Attack Could Trick AI Coding Assistants Into Installing Botnet Malware
blurb: Researchers disclosed HalluSquatting, an attack that exploits the package names AI coding assistants reliably hallucinate: identify the fake name a model invents most often for a trending tool, register it on GitHub or a plugin store, and hide prompt-injection instructions inside. When an assistant with terminal access fetches the trap, the hijacked instructions run attacker code, and researchers reproduced the same wrong name in up to 85% of repository requests.

title: Decoding the obfuscated bash script on a Uniqlo t-shirt
blurb: A developer discovered a base64-encoded, self-evaluating bash script printed on the back of a Uniqlo x Akamai t-shirt, then transcribed and decoded it to recover the Easter egg inside. Base64 has no error correction, so the author ran the shirt through three OCR passes (Android circle-to-search, Tesseract, and Claude), diffed the outputs to catch mismatches, and hand-cleaned the result into a valid shebang script.

title: Benchmarking Coding Agents on Databricks’ Multi-Million Line Codebase
blurb: Databricks benchmarked coding agents on its own multi-million line codebase, evaluating models and harnesses against real tasks its engineers had completed across Python, Go, TypeScript, and Scala. The results clustered tools into three cost-versus-performance tiers, prompting the team to route common operational work like flag flips and config edits to cheaper Haiku and GPT 5.4 Mini class models instead of defaulting to the most expensive ones.

title: Native-speed vLLM transformers modeling backend
blurb: Hugging Face made its transformers modeling backend for vLLM run at near-native speed, letting any of 450+ supported architectures serve through vLLM with a single `--model-impl transformers` flag instead of a hand-written port. The backend composes with tensor, data, and expert parallelism, benchmarked across Qwen3 4B dense, 32B tensor-parallel, and a 235B FP8 MoE on 8×H100, with linear-attention models not yet supported.

title: OpenAI launches GPT-Live voice model series ahead of broad GPT-5.6 release
blurb: OpenAI introduced GPT-Live, a family of voice models with a full-duplex architecture that lets the model interrupt, search the web, or hand off mid-conversation instead of waiting for the user to finish speaking. GPT-Live-1 powers voice mode in paid ChatGPT plans and GPT-Live-1-mini serves the free tier, both routing complex prompts to GPT-5.5 and shipping to developers via API.

title: Introducing Meerkat: an experiment in global consensus
blurb: Cloudflare's Research team built Meerkat, an experimental global consensus service powered by QuePaxa, an EPFL algorithm where every replica can write at any time and progress never stalls on a timeout. This targets the leader-and-timeout failure modes that make Raft brittle across Cloudflare's 330+ data centers, and marks what the team says is QuePaxa's first industrial deployment at global scale.

title: Fake Paysafe, Skrill SDKs on NPM and PyPi steal credentials
blurb: A threat actor published at least 17 malicious npm and PyPI packages impersonating Paysafe, Skrill, and Neteller payment SDKs, exfiltrating API keys, AWS credentials, GitHub tokens, and npm tokens to an AWS-hosted C2 server. The packages expose the expected APIs and return fake success responses instead of hitting Paysafe's backend, with the PyPI variants triggering credential theft on import without needing a Paysafe key present.

title: Malicious AI agent skills can slip past the scanners built to stop them
blurb: Researchers at Hong Kong University of Science and Technology built SkillCloak, a system that hides malicious behavior in AI agent skills while leaving the payload fully functional, and its packing trick evaded more than 90% of the scanners meant to catch it. The finding matters because agent skills run with the coding agent's full privileges, reaching a developer's credentials, SSH keys, and package managers, as the ClawHavoc campaign already demonstrated with over 300 poisoned listings.

title: Rethinking Code Performance Benchmarks for LLMs
blurb: This paper re-evaluates four popular LLM code-efficiency benchmarks (EffiBench, Enamel, EvalPerf, Mercury) by running 1,538 tasks 30 times each with statistical testing, and finds only 6.11% of "performant" implementations are significantly faster than canonical solutions, largely because the benchmark test suites fail to expose real runtime differences. The authors propose a three-agent framework to generate, diagnose, and repair performance-oriented tests, which surfaces significant improvements in roughly 24-25% of previously flat tasks.

title: Finding and Understanding Miscompilation Bugs in the Solidity Compiler
blurb: SolSmith is a semantics-aware differential fuzzer that spent three years finding 25 miscompilation bugs in the Solidity compiler, some latent for years, by generating valid programs that stress code generation and optimization passes. The paper classifies each bug by root cause and end-user impact, exposing pitfalls in the compiler's optimizer that produce silently incorrect code in immutable smart contracts.

title: SpaceXAI launches Grok 4.5 model on Grok Build and APIs
blurb: SpaceXAI released Grok 4.5, its flagship coding model, now live in Grok Build, Cursor on all plans, and the API console with a 500K-token context window, function calling, and configurable reasoning fixed to low, medium, or high. It scores 64.7% on SWE-Bench Pro at $2/$6 per million input/output tokens, and SpaceXAI claims it uses roughly 4.2x fewer output tokens than Opus 4.8 on the same benchmark.

title: Show HN: Rowboat – Open-source, local-first alternative to Claude Desktop
blurb: Rowboat is an open-source, local-first alternative to Claude Desktop that indexes your work into an editable knowledge graph stored as plain Markdown on your machine. It bundles work surfaces including an email client, notes, browser, code mode, and meeting note taker, runs local models via Ollama or LM Studio or hosted models with your own key, and connects to external tools through MCP.

title: Breaking Database Lock-in: Agentic Regeneration of High Performance Storage Readers for Database Bypass
blurb: Jailbreak bypasses the database engine entirely by reading PostgreSQL and MySQL storage files directly, using an LLM to synthesize format-specific readers from source code and documentation rather than routing bulk analytics through JDBC or ODBC. The generated readers emit Apache Arrow buffers consumable by DuckDB, Spark, and cuDF, validated for correctness against driver baselines on TPC-H with up to 27x faster end-to-end analytical throughput.

title: 'GitLost' Flaw Leaks Private Data From GitHub's Agentic Workflows
blurb: Researchers at Noma Security disclosed "GitLost," a prompt injection flaw in GitHub Agentic Workflows that lets an unauthenticated attacker plant instructions in a public repo's Issue and exfiltrate data from an organization's private repos. The workflow's AI agent, backed by Claude or Copilot, treats issue content as instructional input, so any read access it holds across org repositories becomes the attack surface.

title: Experiences with local models for coding
blurb: The author details a second memo on running small models locally for agentic coding on an M3 Max (48GB) and M5 Pro (64GB), structuring the evaluation as a viability funnel from fitting in RAM to acceptable code quality. Each model must clear response speed, tool calling, functional correctness, sustained context, and complex-task handling before the trade-off between coding speed and review effort even becomes worth measuring.

title: Specification Grounding Drives Test Effectiveness for LLM Code
blurb: This paper isolates why LLM self-testing improves code: not the tests existing, but grounding them in the spec. Holding the tester, budget, and repair loop fixed, adding the spec as a checklist yielded correct code 38 percentage points more often across three Claude tiers, replicated on GPT-5.3-codex and Gemini 3.5 Flash, and cut the false-alarm rate from 33% to 0%.

title: Deployment Risk Assessment Using Diff-Aware Features: A Case Study at Prime Video
blurb: Amazon's Prime Video team built a deployment risk classifier that scores code changes using diff-aware features, using LLMs as multi-language feature extractors to avoid language-specific tooling and the privacy concerns of developer metadata. The best model hits 0.83 recall and 0.81 F1 across production and ApacheJIT data, and ablation shows structural code complexity is a far stronger risk signal than lines added or deleted, which turn out to be noisy.

title: AgentTether: Graph-Guided Diagnosis and Runtime Intervention for Reliable LLM Agent Operation
blurb: AgentTether is a runtime repair framework for LLM agents that abstracts each run into a dependency-aware Critical Transition Graph, localizes the failure-critical subtrajectory, and converts it into behavior-scoped guidance that can be applied as live intervention during re-execution. On tau-bench's hardest Banking domain it repairs 59% of failed Qwen3.7-max tasks and 65% of failed GPT-5.4 tasks while cutting agent turns and total tokens, wrapping existing deployments without retraining.

title: Why we built yet another Postgres connection pooler
blurb: PgDog is a Postgres proxy whose connection pooler ships with a built-in SQL parser that tracks per-client SET statements and replays them onto reused server connections, so session state stops leaking between clients. That closes the leaky abstraction behind PgBouncer and RDS Proxy, which force apps to drop SET commands and can silently break RLS policies that depend on session variables.

title: The Art of Computer Programming by Donald E. Knuth
blurb: Knuth's The Art of Computer Programming spans five volumes, now sold as authorized PDFs with clickable cross-references between exercises, answers, index, and text.

title: Show HN: PostgreSQL performance and cost across 23 EC2 instance types
blurb: An interactive tool benchmarks PostgreSQL 17 throughput and cost across 23 EC2 instance types paired with different EBS disks, under a 90% read / 10% write workload at concurrency 32 in us-east-1. You set your data size and required throughput, and it plots every instance × disk combination against a target line so you can pick the cheapest option that clears your requirement.

title: Agents Work Better with Conventional CLIs
blurb: Microsoft tested whether agents drive CLIs more reliably when args are collapsed into a single `--json` payload, benchmarking a synthetic `podctl` tool against a deeply nested deployment spec with arrays, mixed types, and cross-service references. Conventional flag-based args won, since agents handle discrete positional flags more accurately than hand-constructing a large nested JSON blob, where a single malformed quote or misplaced field breaks the entire invocation.

title: How we used AI agents to migrate GitLab rate limiting
blurb: A three-person GitLab pod used the Duo Agent Platform and other AI tools to migrate their legacy rate-limiting system, unifying an application-level limiter with 121 keys and a separate Rack-level path onto a single labkit-ruby implementation. Agents drafted specs, wrote tests, and pre-reviewed merge requests inside a strict read-spec-review-implement-verify loop, while humans kept ownership of scope, architecture, and rollout across 14 specs and 30-plus merge requests.

title: Progressive Crystallization: Turning Agent Exploration into Deterministic, Lower-Cost Workflows in Production
blurb: This paper introduces progressive crystallization, a lifecycle that treats agent exploration as a discovery step and promotes repeatedly validated agent behaviors into cheaper deterministic workflows, while auto-demoting any that regress. On a production cloud networking AIOps system handling tens of thousands of incidents monthly, it raised deterministic execution from 0% to 45% over eight months and cut per-incident agent costs by more than 70% despite doubling volume.

title: Chatto is now open source
blurb: Chatto, a self-hostable group and team chat application, is now open source and installable via Homebrew, running as a single executable that serves its own frontend. It ships with end-to-end encrypted voice, video, and screen-sharing, per-user encryption keys for data at rest, and one process per community with no federation or third-party analytics.

title: Why AI Infrastructure must evolve for Agent Experience — Akshat Bubna, Modal CTO
blurb: Modal CTO Akshat Bubna argues that cloud infrastructure built for humans who read docs and debug from dashboards fails agents, which need tight feedback loops, isolated sandboxes, and machine-operable context to write, run, and retry code. He makes the case that Kubernetes was never designed for bursty compute-heavy workloads, that RL rollouts can demand 100,000 sandboxes, and that observability may matter more than reading code.

title: A bug which affected only left handed users
blurb: A WordPress site owner traced a bug where scrolling on a phone spuriously triggered a comment reply box, but only for users scrolling with their left thumb near the left-aligned "reply" link. The cause was a 2017 touchstart listener added to work around the old 300ms click delay, obsolete since browsers dropped that behavior years earlier, and the fix was deleting a couple of lines.

title: Automating cross-repo documentation with GitHub Agentic Workflows
blurb: The Aspire team built cross-repo documentation automation using GitHub Agentic Workflows, turning merged product pull requests in microsoft/aspire into SME-reviewed docs pull requests in the separate microsoft/aspire.dev repo without granting agents broad write-everywhere tokens. For Aspire 13.3 and 13.4, the setup shipped 82 feature-docs pull requests at a median of 44.8 hours after the product pull request, each reviewed by the engineer who wrote the feature.

title: Local, CPU-Friendly, High-Quality TTS (Text-to-Speech) with Kokoro
blurb: Kokoro is an 82M-parameter TTS model that runs entirely on CPU while producing realistic speech across English, Mandarin, and Hindi with roughly 50 voices. The walkthrough sets it up via the Kokoro-FastAPI container, which exposes an OpenAI-compatible speech endpoint so existing code can swap in local synthesis by pointing at localhost, with the trade-off being a 5GB image.

title: Some Notes on Lambda MicroVMs
blurb: AWS launched Lambda MicroVMs, which run your code in an isolated VM within seconds for up to eight hours, sitting between Lambda functions and EC2 instances and capable of running Docker containers. The author documents the sharp edges: first-class pty access via CreateMicrovmShellAuthToken, blocked outbound UDP that breaks container DNS until you point at Lambda's resolver, and network connectors whose ENIs stay hidden unless you pass DescribeNetworkInterfaces(IncludeManagedResources=true).

title: Expanding Managed Agents in Gemini API:  background tasks, remote MCP and more
blurb: Google expanded Managed Agents in the Gemini Interactions API with background execution, remote MCP server integration, custom function calling, and network credential refresh. Passing `background: true` returns an ID for polling or reconnecting to long-running tasks, while remote MCP support lets agents reach private databases and internal APIs from their sandbox without custom proxy middleware.

title: Biased or Personalized? The Impact of Personal Information on AI-driven Development
blurb: This paper characterizes how AI coding tools can shape generated software around a developer's inferred age and gender rather than the task itself, splitting the effect across interface design, template content, and code structure. Controlled experiments on 800 AI-generated websites found significant differences across all three dimensions, surfacing a personalization-versus-fairness tension in AI-assisted programming that most current tooling does not account for.

title: What Resolve Rate Hides: Trajectory Structure Diagnostics for Coding Agents
blurb: TraceProbe is a trajectory-diagnostic framework that goes beyond resolve rate by normalizing each coding-agent run into a nine-type action taxonomy, then flagging anti-patterns like search loops and aligning run pairs to locate where behavior diverges. Applied to 2,500 SWE-Bench Verified trajectories, it finds that function selection separates success from failure better than file choice, and that even passing runs vary in how fast they reach relevant code.

title: Unicode's transliteration rules are Turing-complete
blurb: Unicode's UTS #35 transliteration rules, the ordered rewrite system ICU ships as locale data across most operating systems, browsers, and databases, are Turing-complete under their unbounded semantics, making termination on a given input undecidable. The author proves it by compiling a 2-tag system into one rule per letter, using the cursor-revisiting feature to rescan freshly written text, and demonstrates the construction running a Collatz machine in PyICU.

title: Bringing PyTorch Monarch to AMD GPUs: Single-Controller Distributed Training on ROCm
blurb: PyTorch ported Monarch, its single-controller distributed training runtime, from CUDA to AMD Instinct GPUs on ROCm, extending actor-based cluster orchestration and fault tolerance beyond NVIDIA hardware. The post details porting Monarch's GPU runtime and communication stack, and shows how the system lets healthy nodes keep training while failed nodes recover and rejoin, avoiding full-job restarts from the last checkpoint.

title: Remote Attestation
blurb: Remote attestation uses a TPM to cryptographically prove a host booted into an expected state, covering hardware, firmware, kernel, init image, and arbitrary custom checks after every reboot. The post explains how measured boot differs from secure boot by verifying signed measurements rather than signed artifacts, then ties attestation to real enforcement by TPM-sealing the root filesystem and refusing mTLS certificates to hosts that fail measurement.

title: Mining Workflow Graphs for Black-Box Boundary Testing of Conversational LLM Agents
blurb: AgentEval is a black-box testing framework that discovers hidden stateful boundaries in conversational LLM agents, such as identity checks and confirmation gates buried behind multi-turn prerequisites. It mines a conversational workflow graph to enumerate specific guards as test targets, covering 23 to 38 boundaries per agent versus 12 for a prompt-only baseline, at lower duplicate and false-alarm rates.

title: Jim's TrueType QR Code Font
blurb: A TrueType font that renders bracket-delimited text like `[hello]` as a scannable QR code via OpenType GSUB shaping, leaving surrounding text readable.

title: Show HN: Davit, a Apple Containers UI
blurb: Davit is a native macOS app for running Linux containers on Apple silicon, talking directly to Apple's open-source container daemon over XPC without Docker Desktop, Electron, or web views. Built in SwiftUI, it handles the full workflow: live per-container stats, streaming logs, interactive shells, filesystem browsing, image builds via Apple's BuildKit, and docker-compose stacks in one click. Free and MIT licensed.

title: Better Auth is joining Vercel
blurb: Better Auth is joining Vercel, folding the open-source, framework-agnostic auth library into Vercel while keeping it open source and platform-agnostic. The deal gives the project more resources to build primitives for agent-driven access, where agents act on a user's behalf and need scoped, revocable permissions, and to spread those primitives across Vercel's products.

title: Not all model upgrades are upgrades
blurb: Benchmarks and per-token pricing made Claude Sonnet 5 look like a clear upgrade over Sonnet 4.6, but across 150 agent tasks it consumed up to 12x more tokens at the median, wiping out its 33% discount and running 3.7x more expensive on code upgrades. Quality also regressed on architecture work, where Sonnet 4.6 scored 90% on idiomatic output versus 78% for the newer model.

title: Postgres Is Enough
blurb: The author argues most teams reach for Redis, Elasticsearch, MongoDB, and Kafka when Postgres already handles caching, full-text search, flexible schemas, queues, and vector embeddings well enough. The case rests on operational cost: every added data store multiplies backup strategies, failure modes, and 3 AM pages, and only about 0.3% of projects ever hit the scale that justifies specialized infrastructure.

title: Ex-GitHub chief’s Entire opens distributed Git network for the AI agent era
blurb: Entire, the startup from former GitHub CEO Thomas Dohmke, launched a preview of a distributed Git network that offloads the concurrent read traffic AI coding agents generate so they stop hitting the rate limits of centralized hosting. Developers mirror an existing GitHub repo in one step and agents clone from a regional mirror, with early tests sustaining about 570,000 clones an hour from a single repo and the Git backend slated to be open-sourced.

title: LogicHunter: Testing LLM Agent Frameworks with an Agentic Oracle
blurb: LogicHunter is a fuzzing framework for testing LLM agent frameworks like LangChain, LlamaIndex, and CrewAI, where defects surface as silent semantic failures rather than crashes, defeating traditional oracles. It pairs specification-driven input generation with an Agentic Oracle that retrieves docs, navigates source, and inspects runtime state via a ReAct loop, finding 40 unknown bugs (30 confirmed, 26 fixed) at 91.17% oracle precision versus 29.27% for the best passive baseline.

title: Get Ready For the Powerful CSS border-shape Property!
blurb: The CSS border-shape property applies clip-path-style values, including the new shape() function, to shape an entire element while letting border, box-shadow, and outline follow that shape rather than the element's rectangular box. Unlike clip-path or mask, which clip the whole element and drop decorations, border-shape preserves borders and shadows along the custom outline, though support is currently Chromium-only.

title: Former GitHub CEO launches competitor designed for the age of vibe coding
blurb: GitHub's former CEO launched a rival code-hosting platform built for AI-generated workloads, arguing incumbent forges were designed for human commit patterns rather than the volume that agentic and vibe coding produce. The pitch lands as GitHub itself struggles to manage AI-driven load, opening room for challengers targeting teams whose repositories are increasingly written and committed by models rather than people.

title: What a harness is and how to build one with Claude Agent SDK
blurb: Claire Vo builds a Sentry bug-debugging harness live using the Claude Agent SDK, an Ink terminal UI, and opinionated adapters for Sentry, Linear, GitHub, and Vercel to automate evidence gathering, root-cause analysis, and follow-up artifacts. The post covers the runs/tasks/tools/artifacts architecture, the three components every harness needs, and when a purpose-built harness beats a general-purpose tool like Claude Code or Codex.

title: What Makes a Good Bug Report for an AI Agent?
blurb: This paper studies which bug-report features actually help LLM repair agents, using 433 SWE-bench Verified issues across 87 agents plus controlled ablations on two models. Fix suggestions, reproduction scripts, and localization cues raised resolution rates while longer reports lowered them, and flattening lists or dropping section headers cut solve rates even when no content was removed.

title: Odin 1.0 Announcement
blurb: Odin reached 1.0, marking the first stable release of the C-alternative systems language built by Ginger Bill around manual memory management, built-in data-oriented features like SOA structs, and a custom context system for allocators. The 1.0 milestone locks the core language and standard library semantics, giving projects that already ship in Odin a stable target instead of tracking a moving pre-release compiler.

title: How GitHub Copilot enables zero DNS configuration for GitHub Pages
blurb: A GitHub walkthrough uses Copilot CLI plus a community Namecheap skill to take a repo from empty to a live custom domain with HTTPS in about 14 minutes, no manual DNS edits. Copilot generates the landing page and enables GitHub Pages, while the Namecheap skill drives A records and CNAME entries through the registrar's API, leaving the developer to approve each step rather than hand-edit records.

title: Reliable and Developer-Aligned Evaluation of Agents for Software Engineering
blurb: This paper proposes an evaluation methodology for LLM coding agents grounded in real-world development rather than synthetic syntactic tasks, built around contamination-awareness, in-the-wild agentic behavior assessment, and trajectory-aware benchmarks. The authors argue existing benchmarks distort true model capabilities, and instead measure realistic coding contexts, human-aligned behavior, and failure modes across the full development cycle.

title: Prompt Coach: An Empirical Evaluation of an Agentic Tutor for Learning Prompt Engineering in Software Development
blurb: Prompt Coach embeds a Socratic agentic tutor in the IDE that scores code-generation prompt quality across multiple dimensions, then surfaces targeted questions grounded in the developer's codebase and the target LLM's behavior to guide self-correction. In a study of 15 professional developers, a single 60-minute session produced statistically significant prompt-quality gains, with the largest improvements on dimensions developers commonly overlook.

title: Geosql: A Claude/Codex skill for geospatial data
blurb: GeoSQL is a skill for Claude, Codex, and GitHub Copilot that lets data scientists run natural-language geospatial queries against PostGIS, BigQuery, Snowflake, and Wherobots, with a map in the agent loop for feedback. It explores warehouse metadata instead of guessing schemas, writes engine-specific spatial SQL, and dry-runs BigQuery queries against a 10 GiB cap, rewriting over-budget ones cheaper rather than executing them.

title: Together for a healthier Clippy
blurb: The Clippy team launched a reciprocal review initiative where @rustbot prompts each contributor who opens a pull request to review someone else's, granting reviewers priority on their own PRs in return. The move responds to a reviewing-capacity shortage, since no Clippy maintainer is currently funded to work on the linter, and takes inspiration from the Bevy project's Open Code Review system.

title: l: A new runtime for k and q
blurb: l is a new runtime for the k and q array languages that runs existing K4/q and qSQL code with zero rewrites.

title: Microsoft releases Visual Studio Code 1.128 with OS-level keyboard shortcuts
blurb: Microsoft released Visual Studio Code 1.128, the latest weekly build, whose headline addition is OS-level keyboard shortcuts that let key bindings register at the operating system layer rather than only within the editor window. The update ships alongside a handful of smaller features, continuing the editor's cadence of incremental weekly releases.

title: Dev ports Linux to Atari's notorious Jaguar console from 1993 — the first 64-bit console features 2MB of RAM, 13.3 MHz CPU, and Tom and Jerry co-processors; the Jag was notoriously difficult to program and flopped
blurb: A Spanish developer ported Linux to Atari's 1993 Jaguar console, working around its 2MB of RAM, 13.3 MHz Motorola 68000 CPU, and lack of an MMU by leaning on uClinux's support for MMU-less 68k systems. The post walks through the RAM and storage optimization plus a custom console driver for the Tom co-processor, ending with a working kernel and BusyBox shell booting on real hardware and in emulation.

title: Theory of constraints, AI, and code review
blurb: The author argues that AI's marginal company-level productivity gains, despite individuals writing code 50% faster, stem from the theory of constraints: a system can't move faster than its slowest stage, and that stage is now code review. As engineers generate more PRs than ever, reviewers drown in a backlog that still caps most teams at one or two shipped changes per day, leaving the real bottleneck untouched.

title: A Global Author-Identity Map for the World of Code:62.7M Developer Identities from 106.8M Author Strings over 5.87B Commits
blurb: A curated author-identity map folding 106.8M commit strings into 62.7M developer identities across World of Code's 5.87B commits.

title: Ubuntu emphasizes Arm64 support – and gets Rustier
blurb: Canonical's latest Ubuntu roadmap elevates Arm64 to a first-class release architecture and pulls more Rust-based utilities into the base system, continuing the replacement of longstanding C coreutils. The Arm64 push targets the growing pool of Ampere and Apple-silicon developer machines, while the Rust shift trades mature GNU tooling for memory-safe rewrites that some admins will need to test against existing scripts.

title: SpaceXAI launches Grok 4.5, touts lower coding-task costs than AI rivals
blurb: SpaceXAI launched Grok 4.5, a model tuned for coding and agentic work at $2 per million input tokens and $6 per million output tokens, running at 80 tokens per second and available in the SpaceXAI console, Grok Build, and Cursor. On Artificial Analysis' Coding Agent Index it lands roughly level with GPT-5.5 in Codex at an estimated $2.49 per task, against $5.07 for Codex and $11.80 for Fable 5 in Claude Code.

title: My Thoughts on the Bun Rust Rewrite
blurb: Zig creator Andrew Kelley responds to Bun's decision to rewrite in Rust, arguing that Bun's trajectory soured once VC funding turned an open source collaboration into a business that demanded a punishing grind from its engineers. He credits Jarred Sumner for years of genuine support to the Zig project, including a $60,000 annual donation, but frames the Rust rewrite as the endpoint of a Silicon Valley mindset that prioritized racing to ship over building with the community.

title: Postgres rewritten in Rust, now passing 100% of the Postgres regression tests
blurb: pgrust, a Postgres rewrite in Rust, passes 100% of Postgres 18.3's 46,000+ regression queries and boots from existing Postgres data directories.

title: Have you heard? Clickhouse is winning the observability wars!
blurb: ClickHouse handles observability workloads at 10TB/day that break Elastic, Grafana's LGTM stack, and Datadog, because scaling it just means adding shards rather than re-architecting around performance cliffs and schema lock-in. Majors argues the real story isn't ClickHouse winning a vendor war but columnar storage making observability a fundamentally different tool than the three-pillars model of separate logs, metrics, and traces. High-cardinality queries stop being a cost problem.

title: Benchmarking Coding Agents on Databricks’ Multi-Million Line Codebase
blurb: Databricks published results from an internal benchmark that scored coding agents on real engineering tasks against its multi-million-line codebase spanning Python, Go, TypeScript, and Scala, finding the models cluster into three cost-performance tiers. The takeaway for the team was to stop defaulting to the most expensive frontier models and route routine work like flag flips and config edits to cheaper Haiku and GPT 5.4 Mini class models.

title: Breaking Database Lock-in: Agentic Regeneration of High Performance Storage Readers for Database Bypass
blurb: This paper introduces Jailbreak, a method that bypasses JDBC/ODBC drivers by reading PostgreSQL and MySQL storage files directly and materializing them as Apache Arrow buffers for engines like DuckDB, Spark, and cuDF. The key move is using LLMs to synthesize format-specific readers from a database's own source code and docs, rather than hand-writing parsers. On TPC-H, the generated readers matched driver-based results while hitting up to 27x throughput gains on read-replica and offline analytics workloads.

title: Spider 2.0-AIFunc: Extending Real-World Text-to-SQL to AI-Native SQL Workflows
blurb: Spider 2.0-AIFunc introduces a 465-instance benchmark testing whether models can generate AI-native SQL, the LLM functions (classification, sentiment, extraction, similarity search) that Snowflake and other warehouses now expose as native calls. The strongest proprietary models hit 67-70% execution accuracy versus 58.1% for the best open-source model, with most errors in predicate specification, schema grounding, and function parameterization. Notably, the agent frameworks built for traditional text-to-SQL don't transfer: a minimal agent setup matches or beats the elaborate ones.

title: Why we built yet another Postgres connection pooler
blurb: PgDog is a new Postgres proxy that adds connection pooling without the usual leaky abstraction, using a built-in SQL parser to track SET statements per client so session state does not leak between pooled connections. That closes the gap that forces teams onto PgBouncer or RDS Proxy to drop SET entirely, which otherwise breaks features like statement timeouts and session-variable-based Row Level Security.

title: Show HN: PostgreSQL performance and cost across 23 EC2 instance types
blurb: A benchmarking project measured PostgreSQL 17 performance and on-demand cost across 23 EC2 instance types, sweeping instance × disk combinations under a 90% read / 10% write workload at concurrency 32. The interactive tool lets you set your data size and throughput target, then surfaces the cheapest instance that clears the line. It turns Postgres sizing on AWS from guesswork into a plot where price and headroom are visible at once.

title: Running Low-Latency Analytical Workloads with GPU-Accelerated Presto on NVIDIA GB200 NVL72
blurb: NVIDIA detailed running GPU-accelerated Presto on GB200 NVL72 and DGX B200, using cuDF algorithms and NVLink 5.0 to beat CPU Presto on TPC-H benchmarks that otherwise required 8-10 nodes of Intel Xeon servers. For data teams weighing warehouse spend, the pitch is collapsing an interactive analytics cluster onto a single GPU node, with GPUDirect Storage handling I/O throughput when paired with IBM Storage Scale.

title: From Custom-Fit to Portable: Bridging the Gap Between Synthesized and Engineered GPU Query Execution
